name: 1 Horse
bio: Most early industrial railways used two sources of power: horses and gravity. Often horses would ride downhill in dandy wagons, to save their energy for pulling the train uphill.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Most early industrial railways used two sources of power: horses and gravity. Often horses would ride downhill in dandy wagons, to save their energy for pulling the train uphill.

name: 2 Horses
bio: The power of a horse-drawn train can easily be doubled by the expedient addition of a second horse.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The power of a horse-drawn train can easily be doubled by the expedient addition of a second horse.

name: "Puffing Billy"
bio: Puffing Billy was the first steam locomotive to operate by the adhesion of the wheels alone, all previous locomotives using a rack by the railside. The real version was very slow, capable only of walking speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Puffing Billy was the first steam locomotive to operate by the adhesion of the wheels alone, all previous locomotives using a rack by the railside. The real version was very slow, capable only of walking speeds.

name: Stephenson "Locomotion No. 1"
bio: Originally named "Active", the Stockton and Darlington's Locomotion No. 1 was the first steam locomotive to operate on a public railway. Unfortunately the early design was flawed and it soon became obsolete.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Originally named "Active", the Stockton and Darlington's Locomotion No. 1 was the first steam locomotive to operate on a public railway. Unfortunately the early design was flawed and it soon became obsolete.

name: Hackworth "Royal George"
bio: Timothy Hackworth was an influential early locomotive engineer. The Royal George locomotive was built around the boiler from an earlier locomotive Chittaprat, and was capable of hauling heavy coal trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}Timothy Hackworth was an influential early locomotive engineer. The Royal George locomotive was built around the boiler from an earlier locomotive Chittaprat, and was capable of hauling heavy coal trains.

name: "Cycloped"
bio: Brandreth's "Cycloped" was the only entry in the Rainhill Trials not powered by steam. Unfortunately during the trials the horse burst through the floor, withdrawing it from the competition.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Brandreth's "Cycloped" was the only entry in the Rainhill Trials not powered by steam. Unfortunately during the trials the horse burst through the floor, withdrawing it from the competition.

name: Stephenson "Rocket"
bio: Although not the first steam locomotive, Rocket's advanced design and success in the Rainhill trials made it the pivotal design for steam engines to replace horses on the railway.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Although not the first steam locomotive, Rocket's advanced design and success in the Rainhill trials made it the pivotal design for steam engines to replace horses on the railway.

name: Stephenson "Planet"
bio: Stephenson's "Planet" locomotives made several improvements to the Rocket design, including being the first locomotive to feature cylinders inside the frame.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Stephenson's "Planet" locomotives made several improvements to the Rocket design, including being the first locomotive to feature cylinders inside the frame.

name: Stephenson "Patentee"
bio: The Patentee was an enlarged version of the Planet type with an additional trailing rear axle, making it more stable and allowing a larger firebox.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Patentee was an enlarged version of the Planet type with an additional trailing rear axle, making it more stable and allowing a larger firebox.

name: Forrester Single
bio: George Forrester and Company built the first passenger tank locomotive to enter service. They were supplied to multiple railways in both tank and tender forms.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}George Forrester and Company built the first passenger tank locomotive to enter service. They were supplied to multiple railways in both tank and tender forms.

name: LMR 0-4-2 "Lion"
bio: The Liverpool and Manchester Railway's "Lion" is most famous for its appearance in the 1953 film "The Titfield Thunderbolt", in which it sustained a dent which can still be seen today.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Liverpool and Manchester Railway's "Lion" is most famous for its appearance in the 1953 film "The Titfield Thunderbolt", in which it sustained a dent which can still be seen today.

name: GWR 2-2-2 "Star"
bio: The first GWR Star class was typical of a 2-2-2 "single" passenger locomotive. They were given common names of stars, such as "Dog Star" and "Shooting Star"
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The first GWR Star class was typical of a 2-2-2 "single" passenger locomotive. They were given common names of stars, such as "Dog Star" and "Shooting Star"

name: Bury "Coppernob"
bio: Bury's bar frame locomotives used iron frames between the wheels to reduce weight. The "Coppernob" was named for its copper-plated firebox.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}Bury's bar frame locomotives used iron frames between the wheels to reduce weight. The "Coppernob" was named for its copper-plated firebox.

name: Crampton Locomotive
bio: Crampton locomotives were designed for a low centre of gravity, to run at higher speeds on standard gauge track. Features included large driving wheels and an oval boiler.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}Crampton locomotives were designed for a low centre of gravity, to run at higher speeds on standard gauge track. Features included large driving wheels and an oval boiler.

additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}

name: GWR 4-2-2 "Iron Duke"
bio: The Iron Duke class was powerful and very fast for its time. Although both leading axles were rigidly mounted, the wide curves and broad gauge of the GWR's track made this less of a problem.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Iron Duke class was powerful and very fast for its time. Although both leading axles were rigidly mounted, the wide curves and broad gauge of the GWR's track made this less of a problem.

name: NER 1001 Class
bio: The NER 1001 Class is typical of a "long boiler" freight engine, with a small firebox mounted behind the rear axle.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The NER 1001 Class is typical of a "long boiler" freight engine, with a small firebox mounted behind the rear axle.

name: LNWR DX Goods
bio: More LNWR DX Goods locomotives were produced than any other steam locomotive class: 943 in total.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}More LNWR DX Goods locomotives were produced than any other steam locomotive class: 943 in total.

name: LNWR Lady of the Lake class
bio: Cast iron coupling rods could not withstand high speeds, so early express locomotives used a large single driving wheel to reduce valve gear speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}Cast iron coupling rods could not withstand high speeds, so early express locomotives used a large single driving wheel to reduce valve gear speeds.

name: Midland Railway 156 Class
bio: The Midland 156 class had a long career. It started as an express engine but as power outputs and speeds on the mainline increased it was relegated to increasingly minor duties, ending up being used on small branch lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Midland 156 class had a long career. It started as an express engine but as power outputs and speeds on the mainline increased it was relegated to increasingly minor duties, ending up being used on small branch lines.

name: GWR 517 Class
bio: The GWR 517 class encompassed a variety of 0-4-2 side tank engines, some having been rebuilt from existing saddle tanks.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR 517 class encompassed a variety of 0-4-2 side tank engines, some having been rebuilt from existing saddle tanks.

name: Midland Railway 700 Class
bio: The 700 class was a widely-produced freight engine. In its later years a sixth of the originally produced locomotives were sold to the Italian State Railway.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The 700 class was a widely-produced freight engine. In its later years a sixth of the originally produced locomotives were sold to the Italian State Railway.

name: GNR Stirling 4-2-2
bio: Patrick Stirling's "singles" (also known as "eight footers" due to the large driving wheels) were designed to haul heavy express trains.

bio: Originally designed to operate commuter services, the small A1 tank locomotives were still used into the 1960s on the Hayling Island branch line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Originally designed to operate commuter services, the small A1 tank locomotives were still used into the 1960s on the Hayling Island branch line.

name: LNWR Precedent Class
bio: F.W. Webb's Precedent class was an express passenger locomotive. A large part of the class was given numbers and names of previously withdrawn locomotives, giving it a fragmented numbering scheme.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}F.W. Webb's Precedent class was an express passenger locomotive. A large part of the class was given numbers and names of previously withdrawn locomotives, giving it a fragmented numbering scheme.

name: NBR Drummond Class C (J32)
bio: Dugald Drummond's C Class used large (for the time) 18 inch cylinders. They survived into LNER ownership as class J32, although rebuilt to Holmes' later design by that point.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Dugald Drummond's C Class used large (for the time) 18 inch cylinders. They survived into LNER ownership as class J32, although rebuilt to Holmes' later design by that point.

name: GER Class Y14
bio: GER's Y14 was a highly successful design, being built in much the same form through to 1913. One was built by the Stratford works in under 10 hours.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}GER's Y14 was a highly successful design, being built in much the same form through to 1913. One was built by the Stratford works in under 10 hours.

name: GWR 2301 Dean Goods
bio: William Dean's 2301 class was the first GWR locomotive to have no outside frame. The light weight made them suited to branch lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}William Dean's 2301 class was the first GWR locomotive to have no outside frame. The light weight made them suited to branch lines.

name: LNWR Improved Precedent Class
bio: The Improved Precedent was built to replace the existing Precedent class locomotives, being classed as a "renewal" as it gave an advantageous tax situation. Some Precedents were also rebuilt as Improved Precedents, these having different-sized frames to the wholly new locomotives.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Improved Precedent was built to replace the existing Precedent class locomotives, being classed as a "renewal" as it gave an advantageous tax situation. Some Precedents were also rebuilt as Improved Precedents, these having different-sized frames to the wholly new locomotives.

name: NBR Holmes Class C (J36)
bio: Matthew Holmes' Class C was an evolution of Drummond's design. They had a long career, becoming LNER class J36 and surviving to the last days of steam on British railways.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Matthew Holmes' Class C was an evolution of Drummond's design. They had a long career, becoming LNER class J36 and surviving to the last days of steam on British railways.

name: GWR 3001 "Achilles"
bio: William Dean's 3001 class was the last of the GWR's 2-2-2 singles. They were rebuilt early on with a 4-2-2 layout to prevent derailments caused by the heavy front axle load.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}William Dean's 3001 class was the last of the GWR's 2-2-2 singles. They were rebuilt early on with a 4-2-2 layout to prevent derailments caused by the heavy front axle load.

name: Midland Railway 115 "Spinner"
bio: The Midland 115 class gained the nickname "Spinner" due to the large single driving wheel. The Midland Railway compensated for the low tractive effort of the 4-2-2 layout by double-heading the trains on heavy loads.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Midland 115 class gained the nickname "Spinner" due to the large single driving wheel. The Midland Railway compensated for the low tractive effort of the 4-2-2 layout by double-heading the trains on heavy loads.

name: Drummond M7 Class
bio: The Drummond M7 was built for the London & South Western railway. Its usefulness saw it survive in operation until well into the BR era.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Drummond M7 was built for the London & South Western railway. Its usefulness saw it survive in operation until well into the BR era.

name: GNR C1 Small Boiler
bio: Henry Ivatt introduced the 4-4-2 Atlantic type to the UK with this design, nicknamed "Klondyke" after the peak of the Klondike Gold Rush of the previous year.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}Henry Ivatt introduced the 4-4-2 Atlantic type to the UK with this design, nicknamed "Klondyke" after the peak of the Klondike Gold Rush of the previous year.

name: Midland Railway 2441 Class
bio: Samuel Johnson's tank engine design were rebuilt later in life by Henry Fowler, providing the inspiration for Fowler's own 3F "Jinty" design.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Samuel Johnson's tank engine design were rebuilt later in life by Henry Fowler, providing the inspiration for Fowler's own 3F "Jinty" design.

name: LSWR F9 "Bug"
bio: The F9 was Dugald Drummond's personal locomotive, allowing him to travel across the LSWR for inspections. After Drummond it only saw occasional use at Southampton Docks.

name: GER F48 Class
bio: On introduction the GER F48 was nominally the most powerful 0-6-0 tender locomotive in Britain. Numerous variations of firebox were tried over the production run.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}On introduction the GER F48 was nominally the most powerful 0-6-0 tender locomotive in Britain. Numerous variations of firebox were tried over the production run.

name: Peckett Saddle Tank
bio: This 0-4-0 saddle tank engine is typical of the many acquired by the GWR from smaller railways during the 1923 grouping of railway companies into the Big Four.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}This 0-4-0 saddle tank engine is typical of the many acquired by the GWR from smaller railways during the 1923 grouping of railway companies into the Big Four.

name: Midland Railway 1000 Class
bio: The 1000 class is an express locomotive with a three-cylinder arrangement, using a high pressure cylinder inside the frame and two low pressure cylinders on the outside.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The 1000 class is an express locomotive with a three-cylinder arrangement, using a high pressure cylinder inside the frame and two low pressure cylinders on the outside.

name: GER Class A55 "Decapod"
bio: GER's prototype Decapod was built to outperform low speed acceleration of electric locomotives, to prevent a rival electric railway being approved by Parliament.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}GER's prototype Decapod was built to outperform low speed acceleration of electric locomotives, to prevent a rival electric railway being approved by Parliament.

bio: The LNER ES1 (ES for Electric Shunter) is designed for hauling heavy freight trains on steep inclines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The LNER ES1 (ES for Electric Shunter) is designed for hauling heavy freight trains on steep inclines.

name: SECR H Class
bio: The South Eastern and Chatham Railway H Class is an 0-4-4T tank engine designed for suburban passenger routes. It was in use until the 1960s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The South Eastern and Chatham Railway H Class is an 0-4-4T tank engine designed for suburban passenger routes. It was in use until the 1960s.

name: GWR 4400 Prairie Tank
bio: The GWR "Small Prairie" tank engines were designed for operating branch lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR "Small Prairie" tank engines were designed for operating branch lines.

name: GWR Star Class
bio: The Star class was the first in a string of successful four cylinder express locomotives built by the GWR.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Star class was the first in a string of successful four cylinder express locomotives built by the GWR.

name: GWR "Great Bear"
bio: The Great Bear was a single locomotive, the first 4-6-2 "Pacific" type built in the UK, which served as the GWR's flagship until 1922 despite disappointing performance and low route availability.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Great Bear was a single locomotive, the first 4-6-2 "Pacific" type built in the UK, which served as the GWR's flagship until 1922 despite disappointing performance and low route availability.

bio: The GCR 9N (later LNER A5) is a 4-6-2 tank locomotive designed for suburban passenger services.

name: GCR Robinson Class 8K
bio: The Robinson 8K is a heavy freight engine based on earlier 0-8-0 designs, with a front pony truck to improve the ride and axle loads at the front of the locomotive.

name: LNER Gresley K2 "Ragtimer"

name: LNWR Claughton Class
bio: The Claughton Class, named after the LNWR's chairman at the time, was unfortunately compromised by the Crewe works being unable to build a large boiler.

name: GCR Robinson 9P
bio: Robinson's 9P was a four-cylinder express locotive introduced toward the end of his time at the GCR. Early versions suffered from high coal consumption due to narrow steam passages.

name: GER D81 Class
bio: The D81 (later LNER J20 Class) continued the GER's habit of building powerful 0-6-0s, and remained the most powerful British 0-6-0 tender locomotive for over twenty years.

bio: The LNWR G2 is an 0-8-0 freight locomotive, built from scratch as a new class and never rebuilt instead of the usual LNWR practice of rebuilding existing 8-coupled locomotives.

bio: The 9Q is a freight adaptation of the 9P, which had an exceptional turn of speed for a high tractive effort engine. It earnt the name "Black Pig" for its heavy coal consumption.

name: LNWR 380 Class
bio: The LNWR 380 class is a large tank locomotive with an 0-8-4T chassis. It was derived from the LNWR's 0-8-0 tender engines and similar 0-8-2T tanks.

name: Fowler 3F "Jinty"
bio: The Jinty is a popular tank engine that was built in large quantities. Although small and low powered it is reliable and cheap to run. Jinties were sometimes run in pairs (or more) for working on steep inclines.

name: LNER U1
bio: The largest British steam locomotive ever built, the one-off U1 was designed by Sir Nigel Gresley as a banking engine for the Woodhead line. Although powerful it struggled to find a use after the Woodhead line was electrified, being scrapped in 1955.

name: LMS Hughes Crab
bio: The Hughes Crab was named for its distinctive "scuttling" motion, caused by the inclined cylinders.

name: LNER Gresley D49
bio: The D49 is a Nigel Gresley designed 4-4-0 locomotive for passenger traffic.

name: GWR King Class
bio: The King Class is a larger and more powerful development of the Castle Class, designed for even higher sustained speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The King Class is a larger and more powerful development of the Castle Class, designed for even higher sustained speeds.

name: LMS Fowler 2-6-4T
bio: Fowler's 2-6-4T achieved high speeds for a tank engine, and was typically used on longer distance services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Fowler's 2-6-4T achieved high speeds for a tank engine, and was typically used on longer distance services.

name: LMS Beyer Garratt
bio: The LMS Beyer Garratt was the most-produced Garratt type locomotive, designed for hauling heavy freight. While they suffered from poor fuel efficiency and high maintenance requirements, they survived in service to the late 1950s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.2 tiles{}{SILVER}The LMS Beyer Garratt was the most-produced Garratt type locomotive, designed for hauling heavy freight. While they suffered from poor fuel efficiency and high maintenance requirements, they survived in service to the late 1950s.

name: GWR Hall Class
bio: The GWR 4900 Hall Class set the template for the 4-6-0 mixed traffic locomotive, fast enough for express passenger services with enough tractive effort for freight.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR 4900 Hall Class set the template for the 4-6-0 mixed traffic locomotive, fast enough for express passenger services with enough tractive effort for freight.

name: LNER Gresley A3
bio: The Gresley A3 is a Pacific (4-6-2) locomotive famed for its 100mph+ performance hauling LNER's Flying Scotsman service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Gresley A3 is a Pacific (4-6-2) locomotive famed for its 100mph+ performance hauling LNER's Flying Scotsman service.

name: LMS Patriot Class
bio: The Patriot class combined the Royal Scot chassis with boilers from the LNWR's Large Claughton class. Although none were preserved, there is a project to build a new Patriot-class locomotive.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Patriot class combined the Royal Scot chassis with boilers from the LNWR's Large Claughton class. Although none were preserved, there is a project to build a new Patriot-class locomotive.

name: GWR 5700 Pannier Tank
bio: The 5700 class numbered 863 locomotives, and was ubiquitous across the GWR network.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The 5700 class numbered 863 locomotives, and was ubiquitous across the GWR network.

name: SR Schools Class
bio: The Schools class was the most powerful 4-4-0 locomotive produced in  Europe, and the last British locomotive with such a wheel layout.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Schools class was the most powerful 4-4-0 locomotive produced in  Europe, and the last British locomotive with such a wheel layout.

name: GWR 1400 Class
bio: The 1400 class (originally the 4800 class) is an update of an 1868 design with more modern features. Although small, it is capable of high speed.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The 1400 class (originally the 4800 class) is an update of an 1868 design with more modern features. Although small, it is capable of high speed.

name: LMS Stanier Class 5
bio: The Stanier Class 5, commonly known as the "Black 5", was a workhorse for the LMS and into the British Rail era. Although intended as a mixed traffic locomotive it was capable of high top speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Stanier Class 5, commonly known as the "Black 5", was a workhorse for the LMS and into the British Rail era. Although intended as a mixed traffic locomotive it was capable of high top speeds.

name: LMS Stanier Jubilee
bio: The Jubilee is the counterpart to the Class 5, often called the "Red Stanier" due to its red LMS livery. It was intended for passenger work, with slightly larger diameter driving wheels giving a higher top speed.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Jubilee is the counterpart to the Class 5, often called the "Red Stanier" due to its red LMS livery. It was intended for passenger work, with slightly larger diameter driving wheels giving a higher top speed.

name: LMS Stanier 8F
bio: The Stanier 8F is a further development of the Class 5, specialised for freight traffic with a 2-8-0 layout.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Stanier 8F is a further development of the Class 5, specialised for freight traffic with a 2-8-0 layout.

name: LNER Gresley A4
bio: The Gresley A4 is the iconic pinnacle of high-speed British steam, holding numerous speed and power records.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Gresley A4 is the iconic pinnacle of high-speed British steam, holding numerous speed and power records.

name: LNER Gresley V2
bio: The Gresley V2 is derived from the A3 type, adapted to mixed traffic with a shorter boiler, smaller driving wheels and a 2-6-2 layout.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Gresley V2 is derived from the A3 type, adapted to mixed traffic with a shorter boiler, smaller driving wheels and a 2-6-2 layout.

name: Ruston and Hornsby 48DS
bio: A “Really Useless Engine” – these small, simple industrial locomotives saw service in many industrial yards but were almost never seen on the boader network.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}A “Really Useless Engine” – these small, simple industrial locomotives saw service in many industrial yards but were almost never seen on the boader network.

name: Ruston and Hornsby 88DS
bio: A mild upgrade in power made the Ruston 88 significantly more effective than it’s little brother, without vastly increasing cost.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}A mild upgrade in power made the Ruston 88 significantly more effective than it’s little brother, without vastly increasing cost.

name: GWR 6800 Grange Class
bio: The Grange class is a conservative design similar to the Hall class with smaller driving wheels, but proved to be reliable in service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Grange class is a conservative design similar to the Hall class with smaller driving wheels, but proved to be reliable in service.

name: LMS Stanier Coronation
bio: The Stanier Coronation briefly held the British speed record for a steam locomotive. In real life, the streamlined casing was only fitted to early examples.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Stanier Coronation briefly held the British speed record for a steam locomotive. In real life, the streamlined casing was only fitted to early examples.

name: SR Merchant Navy Class
bio: The SR Merchant Navy is a large, powerful locomotive with an air-smoothed casing. Bulleid's novel design features proved to be troublesome, and the class was later rebuilt.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The SR Merchant Navy is a large, powerful locomotive with an air-smoothed casing. Bulleid's novel design features proved to be troublesome, and the class was later rebuilt.

name: SR CC Electric
bio: With six traction motors, the CC is adept at handling both heavy freight and long passenger trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}With six traction motors, the CC is adept at handling both heavy freight and long passenger trains.

name: SR Bulleid Q1
bio: The Q1 was designed for function over form and the most efficient possible use of materials.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Q1 was designed for function over form and the most efficient possible use of materials.

name: LNER Thompson B1
bio: LNER's B2 was a mixed traffic locomotive, the early examples of which were named after breeds of antelope, with 61018 "Gnu" having the record for shortest name given to a British locomotive.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}LNER's B2 was a mixed traffic locomotive, the early examples of which were named after breeds of antelope, with 61018 "Gnu" having the record for shortest name given to a British locomotive.

name: Hunslet Austerity 0-6-0ST
bio: Hunslet's Austerity was designed for shunting duties. It's powerful for its size with good adhesion, but does not run well at higher speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Hunslet's Austerity was designed for shunting duties. It's powerful for its size with good adhesion, but does not run well at higher speeds.

name: WD Austerity 2-8-0
bio: Robert Riddles adapted the LMS 8F for low cost and simple construction, resulting in this "austerity" freight locomotive.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Robert Riddles adapted the LMS 8F for low cost and simple construction, resulting in this "austerity" freight locomotive.

name: GWR Modified Hall Class
bio: While externally identical to the original Hall class, the Modified Hall was completely new on the inside with many design innovations.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}While externally identical to the original Hall class, the Modified Hall was completely new on the inside with many design innovations.

name: GWR County Class
bio: The County Class was  Frederick Hawksworth's test of design ideas for a new Pacific. Sadly there was not enough demand for express steam locomotives in the late 1940s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The County Class was  Frederick Hawksworth's test of design ideas for a new Pacific. Sadly there was not enough demand for express steam locomotives in the late 1940s.

name: SR West Country Class
bio: Bulleid's "Light Pacific" is a smaller and lighter version of the Merchant Navy, capable of operating on more routes.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}Bulleid's "Light Pacific" is a smaller and lighter version of the Merchant Navy, capable of operating on more routes.

name: SR Bulleid Leader
bio: The Leader was an attempt to make a steam locomotive as easy to operate as a diesel or electric. Complexity, poor reliability and uncomfortably hot cabs caused the project to be scrapped by 1951.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Leader was an attempt to make a steam locomotive as easy to operate as a diesel or electric. Complexity, poor reliability and uncomfortably hot cabs caused the project to be scrapped by 1951.

name: LMS Ivatt Class 2
bio: The Ivatt Class 2 is a light, low-powered Mogul designed to replace the LMS fleet of 0-6-0 engines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Ivatt Class 2 is a light, low-powered Mogul designed to replace the LMS fleet of 0-6-0 engines.

name: EE 10000
bio: The EE 10000 is the first mainline diesel locomotive built in the UK. Two were built for the LMS.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The EE 10000 is the first mainline diesel locomotive built in the UK. Two were built for the LMS.

name: LNER Peppercorn A1
bio: The Peppercorn A1 is designed for hauling heavy passenger trains of 12 or more carriages. A continuation of the series, 'Tornado', was built in 2008.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Peppercorn A1 is designed for hauling heavy passenger trains of 12 or more carriages. A continuation of the series, 'Tornado', was built in 2008.

name: Brown, Boverie & Cie 18000
bio: Originally ordered by the GWR, the 18000 used a gas turbine to match the power output of the King Class. This resulted in it being unreliable and expensive to run.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Originally ordered by the GWR, the 18000 used a gas turbine to match the power output of the King Class. This resulted in it being unreliable and expensive to run.

name: BR Class 76 (EM1)
bio: The Class 76 is an electric freight locomotive designed for the Woodhead Line in the northern Peak District. With high tractive effort, electric traction lent itself to the steep gradients and many tunnels of the line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 76 is an electric freight locomotive designed for the Woodhead Line in the northern Peak District. With high tractive effort, electric traction lent itself to the steep gradients and many tunnels of the line.

name: BR Standard Class 6 "Clan"
bio: The Class 6 is a lighter version of the Class 7 "Britannia", with a smaller boiler. Only 10 were produced due to steel shortages.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 6 is a lighter version of the Class 7 "Britannia", with a smaller boiler. Only 10 were produced due to steel shortages.

name: BR Standard Class 7 "Britannia"
bio: The Class 7 combines the best features of pre-BR designs from the Big Four, with the aim of reducing maintenance costs.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 7 combines the best features of pre-BR designs from the Big Four, with the aim of reducing maintenance costs.

name: BR Class 08
bio: The Class 08 is a diesel-electric shunter that was common across the UK when freight trains were assembled by shunting different consists of wagons. Although slow its low cost and high tractive effort make it a good option for short industrial lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 08 is a diesel-electric shunter that was common across the UK when freight trains were assembled by shunting different consists of wagons. Although slow its low cost and high tractive effort make it a good option for short industrial lines.

name: BR Class 77 (EM2)
bio: The Class 77  is a larger version of the earlier Class 76, designed to haul passenger services on the Woodhead Line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 77  is a larger version of the earlier Class 76, designed to haul passenger services on the Woodhead Line.

name: BR Class 9F
bio: The 9F class included the last steam locomotive ever to be built by British Railways, "Evening Star"
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The 9F class included the last steam locomotive ever to be built by British Railways, "Evening Star"

name: EE Class 55 "Deltic"
bio: The Class 55 uses two Napier Deltic marine engines to create a high speed express diesel locomotive.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 55 uses two Napier Deltic marine engines to create a high speed express diesel locomotive.

name: Brush Class 31 "Gurgler"
bio: The Class 31 (Brush Type 2) was part of BR's pilot scheme to test replacing steam locomotives with diesel traction. It had many nicknames including "Ped", "Gargoyle" and "Gurgler"
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 31 (Brush Type 2) was part of BR's pilot scheme to test replacing steam locomotives with diesel traction. It had many nicknames including "Ped", "Gargoyle" and "Gurgler"

name: BR Class 03
bio: The Class 03 is a diesel-mechanical shunter, with a five speed manual gearbox driven through a fluid coupling. They were most useful in areas where the Class 08 was too large and heavy to operate.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 03 is a diesel-mechanical shunter, with a five speed manual gearbox driven through a fluid coupling. They were most useful in areas where the Class 08 was too large and heavy to operate.

name: EE Class 20 "Chopper"
bio: The Class 20 (English Electric Type 1) is a diesel-electric locomotive designed for light freight duties. Due to its simple design it was more reliable than many contemporary locomotives, leading to a large number being built.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 20 (English Electric Type 1) is a diesel-electric locomotive designed for light freight duties. Due to its simple design it was more reliable than many contemporary locomotives, leading to a large number being built.

name: GT3 Tender
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}

name: EE GT3
bio: The GT3 was part of a programme to test gas turbine locomotives. It used a conventional steam driving wheel arrangement to keep the design simple.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The GT3 was part of a programme to test gas turbine locomotives. It used a conventional steam driving wheel arrangement to keep the design simple.

name: BR Class 42 "Warship"
bio: The Class 42 is based on the German V200 design, scaled down to fit the smaller British loading gauge and built under licence by British manufacturers.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 42 is based on the German V200 design, scaled down to fit the smaller British loading gauge and built under licence by British manufacturers.

name: EE Class 40 "Whistler"
bio: Initially unwanted due to the small improvement in performance over the Class 7 Britannia, the Class 40 (English Electric Type 4) found its home on the West Coast Main Line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}Initially unwanted due to the small improvement in performance over the Class 7 Britannia, the Class 40 (English Electric Type 4) found its home on the West Coast Main Line.

name: BR Class 28 "Metrovick"
bio: The Class 28 features some unusual design elements, with a two-stroke diesel engine and a "Co-Bo" layout with five axles: three at the front and two at the rear. It was sadly unreliable in use, with engine problems and a tendency for the cab windows to fall out.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 28 features some unusual design elements, with a two-stroke diesel engine and a "Co-Bo" layout with five axles: three at the front and two at the rear. It was sadly unreliable in use, with engine problems and a tendency for the cab windows to fall out.

name: BR Class 71 (HA)
bio: The Class 71 is a mixed-traffic electric locomotive designed to work on the third rail system. It offered a lot of power for a small locomotive, but being confined to third rail power with no auxiliary option limited its usefulness.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 71 is a mixed-traffic electric locomotive designed to work on the third rail system. It offered a lot of power for a small locomotive, but being confined to third rail power with no auxiliary option limited its usefulness.

name: BRCW Class 26 "Teacup"
bio: The Class 26 "Teacup" (so named for its idling sound) was an early Type 2 locomotive, small diesels with engine output between 800 and 1,250 hp.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 26 "Teacup" (so named for its idling sound) was an early Type 2 locomotive, small diesels with engine output between 800 and 1,250 hp.

name: EE Class 23 "Baby Deltic"
bio: The Class 23 uses a smaller version of the Napier Deltic engine fitted to the Class 55, leading to its nickname of "Baby Deltic".
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 23 uses a smaller version of the Napier Deltic engine fitted to the Class 55, leading to its nickname of "Baby Deltic".

name: AEI Class 81 (AL1)
bio: Built by Associated Electrical Industries, the Class 81 was British Railways' first AC electric locomotive. Two types were envisioned, but all delivered were geared for 100mph passenger and express freight usage.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Built by Associated Electrical Industries, the Class 81 was British Railways' first AC electric locomotive. Two types were envisioned, but all delivered were geared for 100mph passenger and express freight usage.

name: EE Class 37 "Tractor"
bio: The Class 37 (English Electric Type 3) is widely used, especially in Scotland where the high power, low axle load and lack of need for electrification make it ideal for hauling long trains in the Highlands.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 37 (English Electric Type 3) is widely used, especially in Scotland where the high power, low axle load and lack of need for electrification make it ideal for hauling long trains in the Highlands.

name: EE Class 37/6 "Tractor"
bio: Originally to power the “Nightstar” european sleeper services away from electrified lines, this sub-class of 12 locomotives mostly saw use as rescue and freight locomotives with other operators.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Originally to power the “Nightstar” european sleeper services away from electrified lines, this sub-class of 12 locomotives mostly saw use as rescue and freight locomotives with other operators.

name: Sulzer Class 45 "Peak"
bio: The Sulzer Peaks served express passenger trains on the Midland Main Line until the introduction of the HST.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Sulzer Peaks served express passenger trains on the Midland Main Line until the introduction of the HST.

name: BRCW Class 33 "Crompton"
bio: The Class 33 was built without carriage heating, using the space for an 8 cylinder engine. This meant it was used for tourist-loaded passenger trains in summer, and freight in winter.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 33 was built without carriage heating, using the space for an 8 cylinder engine. This meant it was used for tourist-loaded passenger trains in summer, and freight in winter.

name: BR Class 35 "Hymek"
bio: The Hymeks were named for their hydraulic transmission. Sadly this also caused their early withdrawal, with BR viewing diesel-hydraulics as non-standard.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Hymeks were named for their hydraulic transmission. Sadly this also caused their early withdrawal, with BR viewing diesel-hydraulics as non-standard.

name: BR Class 52 "Western"
bio: The Class 52 was the most powerful of the Western Region's diesel-hydraulic locomotives, pushing the limits of what 1960s transmission technology could achieve.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 52 was the most powerful of the Western Region's diesel-hydraulic locomotives, pushing the limits of what 1960s transmission technology could achieve.

name: Brush Class 53 "Falcon"
bio: Brush's "Falcon" was a prototype for a new generation of Type 4 locomotives, but advances in single-engined diesel made the Falcon with its two Maybach diesels obsolete at the point of introduction.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}Brush's "Falcon" was a prototype for a new generation of Type 4 locomotives, but advances in single-engined diesel made the Falcon with its two Maybach diesels obsolete at the point of introduction.

name: Sulzer Class 25 "Rat"
bio: The Class 25 locomotives became known as "Rats" because they were found all over the British Rail network.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 25 locomotives became known as "Rats" because they were found all over the British Rail network.

name: Brush Class 47 "Spoon"
bio: The Class 47 diesel-electric arose from BR's desire for a lighter Type 4 (over 2000hp) locomotive, without using the diesel-hydraulic technology favoured by the Western Region.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 47 diesel-electric arose from BR's desire for a lighter Type 4 (over 2000hp) locomotive, without using the diesel-hydraulic technology favoured by the Western Region.

name: Clayton Class 17
bio: The Class 17 attempted to solve the problems of visibility from a single cab. It had an unsuccessful life beset by engine reliability problems, and all examples were withdrawn early.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 17 attempted to solve the problems of visibility from a single cab. It had an unsuccessful life beset by engine reliability problems, and all examples were withdrawn early.

name: BR Class 14 "Teddy Bear"
bio: BR envisaged a need  for small diesel locomotives to run short-distance freight trains, but by the end of the 1960s this need had evaporated. The Class 14s were sold to industry, and saw continued use on private railways.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}BR envisaged a need  for small diesel locomotives to run short-distance freight trains, but by the end of the 1960s this need had evaporated. The Class 14s were sold to industry, and saw continued use on private railways.

name: BR Class 73 (JB)
bio: The Class 73 is a dual-power locomotive, running at full power when connected to a third rail supply but carrying a small diesel engine for running on non-electrified track.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 73 is a dual-power locomotive, running at full power when connected to a third rail supply but carrying a small diesel engine for running on non-electrified track.

name: BR Class 86 (AL6)
bio: The Class 86 (originally AL6) was BR's first standard electric locomotive, incorporating experience from prototypes such as the Class 81.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 86 (originally AL6) was BR's first standard electric locomotive, incorporating experience from prototypes such as the Class 81.

name: EE Class 50 "Hoover"
bio: The Class 50 hauled express services on the West Coast Main Line until electrification, at which point they were used to replace diesel-hydraulic engines in the Western Region.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 50 hauled express services on the West Coast Main Line until electrification, at which point they were used to replace diesel-hydraulic engines in the Western Region.

name: Brush HS4000 "Kestrel"
bio: The Brush "Kestrel" was a prototype high speed diesel locomotive. After a brief period of service in the UK it was sold to the Soviet Union, where it was re-gauged and used for testing.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Brush "Kestrel" was a prototype high speed diesel locomotive. After a brief period of service in the UK it was sold to the Soviet Union, where it was re-gauged and used for testing.

name: BREL Class 87
bio: The Class 87 was a further development of the Class 86 with even more power and performance, serving as the flagship of the British Rail electric fleet.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 87 was a further development of the Class 86 with even more power and performance, serving as the flagship of the British Rail electric fleet.

name: BREL Class 43 HST
bio: The Class 43 HST is a high-speed diesel-electric locomotive, also known as the Intercity 125 for its 125mph service speed and intended use on long routes between cities.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 43 HST is a high-speed diesel-electric locomotive, also known as the Intercity 125 for its 125mph service speed and intended use on long routes between cities.

name: BREL Class 43 “HST” (Buffers)
bio: To facilitate the testing and introduction of the Class 91 fleet prior to the availability of Mark 4 Driving Van Trailers, 8 HST Power Cars were converted to act as control vehicles for electric locomotives.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}To facilitate the testing and introduction of the Class 91 fleet prior to the availability of Mark 4 Driving Van Trailers, 8 HST Power Cars were converted to act as control vehicles for electric locomotives.

name: BREL Class 43 DVT (Buffers)
bio: A depowered Class 43, proposed as a conversion from surplus power cars and now suitable for carrying baggage.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}A depowered Class 43, proposed as a conversion from surplus power cars and now suitable for carrying baggage.

name: BREL E-HST
bio: A proposed electric version of the Class 43 ‘HST’ Power Car was oft-discussed after the failure of the APT program.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}A proposed electric version of the Class 43 ‘HST’ Power Car was oft-discussed after the failure of the APT program.

name: BREL Class 56 "Grid"
bio: The Class 56 is designed for heavy freight work. It contains many improvements to the electric drivetrain compared to earlier diesel-electric locomotives, making it more reliable and easier to operate.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 56 is designed for heavy freight work. It contains many improvements to the electric drivetrain compared to earlier diesel-electric locomotives, making it more reliable and easier to operate.

name: BREL Class 58 "Bone"
bio: The Class 58 used a modular design with replaceable modules mounted to an underframe. It was nicknamed "Bone" after the narrow central section.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 58 used a modular design with replaceable modules mounted to an underframe. It was nicknamed "Bone" after the narrow central section.

name: EMD Class 59
bio: The Class 59 is the first US-built locomotive to operate in mainland Great Britain. It is derived from the SD40, with a British cab layout.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 59 is the first US-built locomotive to operate in mainland Great Britain. It is derived from the SD40, with a British cab layout.

name: BREL Class 88
bio: The Class 88 was a proposed re-use of the Class 58 design, dropping the diesel engine for a 25kv Overhead power supply.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 88 was a proposed re-use of the Class 58 design, dropping the diesel engine for a 25kv Overhead power supply.

name: BREL Class 38 "Mini Bone"
bio: The Class 38 was a proposed cut-down Class 58 using a 2000hp-class diesel engine. If built, these would likely have displaced the oldest remaining Type 2 and Type 3 locomotives on the network, replacing Class 31s and Class 37s on freight duties.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 38 was a proposed cut-down Class 58 using a 2000hp-class diesel engine. If built, these would likely have displaced the oldest remaining Type 2 and Type 3 locomotives on the network, replacing Class 31s and Class 37s on freight duties.

name: BREL APT-S Driving Motor
bio: The APT-S was a rework of the APT concept towards more conventional rolling stock, with a driving motor car, articulated trailers and a DVT.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The APT-S was a rework of the APT concept towards more conventional rolling stock, with a driving motor car, articulated trailers and a DVT.

name: BREL APT-S/Q Driving Van Trailer
bio: The APT-Q was proposed to rationalise existing APT-P stock into usable 6-car tains, with a single non-driving power car and a new-build DVT.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The APT-Q was proposed to rationalise existing APT-P stock into usable 6-car tains, with a single non-driving power car and a new-build DVT.

name: BR Class 89 "Badger"
bio: The Class 89 was a prototype express locomotive; unfortunately the specification changed after the prototype was built, and only a single example was completed. It was withdrawn from service twice after major breakdowns.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 89 was a prototype express locomotive; unfortunately the specification changed after the prototype was built, and only a single example was completed. It was withdrawn from service twice after major breakdowns.

name: BREL Class 90
bio: The Class 90 is a powerful electric locomotive. Initially used for Intercity express services, several were also used for freight.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 90 is a powerful electric locomotive. Initially used for Intercity express services, several were also used for freight.

name: BREL Class 91
bio: The Class 91 is a high speed electric locomotive, designed for Intercity 225 services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 91 is a high speed electric locomotive, designed for Intercity 225 services.

name: Brush Class 60 "Tug"
bio: The Brush Class 60 is a Type 5 diesel, designed for high power and tractive effort to haul heavy freight trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Brush Class 60 is a Type 5 diesel, designed for high power and tractive effort to haul heavy freight trains.

name: MaK Class 21/9
bio: Eurotunnel bought five MaK DE 1004 locomotives for use within the tunnel as service trains, later expanding the fleet with similar DE6400 locomotives bought from DB Schenker Nederland. When running on the UK mainline they are classified as Class 21/9.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Eurotunnel bought five MaK DE 1004 locomotives for use within the tunnel as service trains, later expanding the fleet with similar DE6400 locomotives bought from DB Schenker Nederland. When running on the UK mainline they are classified as Class 21/9.

name: ABB Class 92 "Caravan"
bio: The Class 92 was designed to operate services through the Channel Tunnel. It can run on both the Southern Region third rail system and overhead wires.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 92 was designed to operate services through the Channel Tunnel. It can run on both the Southern Region third rail system and overhead wires.

name: EMD Class 66
bio: After the successful introduction of the Class 59, EWS approached GM's Electro-Motive diesel with a huge order of 250 locomotives which would become Class 66.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}After the successful introduction of the Class 59, EWS approached GM's Electro-Motive diesel with a huge order of 250 locomotives which would become Class 66.

name: Brush Class 57 "Bodysnatcher"
bio: The Class 57 is a re-engineered locomotive using Class 47 bodyshells and parts, giving rise to its "Bodysnatcher" nickname.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 57 is a re-engineered locomotive using Class 47 bodyshells and parts, giving rise to its "Bodysnatcher" nickname.

name: Alstom Class 67 "Skip"
bio: The Class 67 is a high speed freight locomotive designed for mail service. It uses EMD engines, generators and traction motors.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 67 is a high speed freight locomotive designed for mail service. It uses EMD engines, generators and traction motors.

name: GE Class 70
bio: The General Electric Class 70 is designed with crash safety in mind, hence the bonnet-like structure at each end.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The General Electric Class 70 is designed with crash safety in mind, hence the bonnet-like structure at each end.

name: Stadler Class 68 "Warskip"
bio: The Class 68 is a modern mixed traffic disel locomotive with a powerful 16-cylinder Caterpillar engine.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 68 is a modern mixed traffic disel locomotive with a powerful 16-cylinder Caterpillar engine.

name: BR Class 73/9 “Ultra73”
bio: These re-engined and modernized Class 73s retained their ability to run from the third rail but received diesel engines capable of operating at an equivalent power level.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}These re-engined and modernized Class 73s retained their ability to run from the third rail but received diesel engines capable of operating at an equivalent power level.

name: Stadler Class 88
bio: The Class 88 is a dual mode version of Stadler's Euro Dual family, sharing many components with the class 68.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 88 is a dual mode version of Stadler's Euro Dual family, sharing many components with the class 68.

name: Progress Rail Class 69
bio: The Class 69 is a rebuild of the Class 56 to cost-effectively provide a new locomotive with similar performance to the Class 66, which could no longer be purchased due to new rail equipment regulations.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 69 is a rebuild of the Class 56 to cost-effectively provide a new locomotive with similar performance to the Class 66, which could no longer be purchased due to new rail equipment regulations.

name: Stadler Class 93
bio: The Class 88 is a dual mode version of Stadler's Euro Dual family, sharing many components with the class 68.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 88 is a dual mode version of Stadler's Euro Dual family, sharing many components with the class 68.

name: Stadler Class 99
bio: The Class 88 is a dual mode version of Stadler's Euro Dual family, sharing many components with the class 68.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 88 is a dual mode version of Stadler's Euro Dual family, sharing many components with the class 68.

name: BREL Mk2 DBSO
bio: The DBSO is a driving trailer built from a converted Mk2F carriage. They were first used with Class 47 locomotives for a push-pull service that could be driven from either end.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The DBSO is a driving trailer built from a converted Mk2F carriage. They were first used with Class 47 locomotives for a push-pull service that could be driven from either end.

name: BR Mark 3 Driving Trailer
bio: A conversion of the Mk3 coach to incorporate a driving cab was proposed before the Polmont derailment put a stop to the idea of having passengers in the leading vehicle of locomotive-propelled trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}A conversion of the Mk3 coach to incorporate a driving cab was proposed before the Polmont derailment put a stop to the idea of having passengers in the leading vehicle of locomotive-propelled trains.

name: Mk3 Driving Van Trailer
bio: This driving van trailer can be used with diesel and electric locomotives, either as a leading or trailing vehicle. It is recommended for use with the Class 91.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}This driving van trailer can be used with diesel and electric locomotives, either as a leading or trailing vehicle. It is recommended for use with the Class 91.

name: Mk4 Driving Van Trailer
bio: This driving van trailer can be used with diesel and electric locomotives, either as a leading or trailing vehicle. It is recommended for use with the Class 91.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}This driving van trailer can be used with diesel and electric locomotives, either as a leading or trailing vehicle. It is recommended for use with the Class 91.

name: CAF Mk5A DVT
bio: Mark 5A carriage sets include a driving van trailer, enabling loco-hauled trains to be driven from both ends.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mark 5A carriage sets include a driving van trailer, enabling loco-hauled trains to be driven from both ends.

name: NER Autocar
bio: The NER Autocar is a petrol-electric railcar with a petrol engine driving two electric motors. It was the first use of an internal combustion engine in a railway vehicle.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The NER Autocar is a petrol-electric railcar with a petrol engine driving two electric motors. It was the first use of an internal combustion engine in a railway vehicle.

name: GWR Railcar
bio: The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body.

name: GWR Railcar (Mail)
bio: The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body. Units 17 and 34 were built for express parcels service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body. Units 17 and 34 were built for express parcels service.

name: GWR Railcar
bio: The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body. Railcars 35 to 38 were built as power twin pairs.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body. Railcars 35 to 38 were built as power twin pairs.

name: GWR Railcar
bio: The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body. Railcars 35 to 38 were built as power twin pairs and were capable of being strengthened with additional carriages.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR Railcars used AEC bus hardware in a lightweight streamlined body. Railcars 35 to 38 were built as power twin pairs and were capable of being strengthened with additional carriages.

name: Metro-Cammell Class 101
bio: The Class 101 has a four speed gearbox with a manual shift. It was exceptionally long-lived, the final examples being retired in December 2003.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 101 has a four speed gearbox with a manual shift. It was exceptionally long-lived, the final examples being retired in December 2003.

name: Midland Railway EMU (1 Car)
bio: Very early experiments in AC Multiple Units were built for the Lancaster-Morecambe-Heysham line, and were remarkably successful. Various lengths of unit were available, with twice as many single-ended driving trailers built as double-ended driving motors were.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Very early experiments in AC Multiple Units were built for the Lancaster-Morecambe-Heysham line, and were remarkably successful. name: LB&SCR “CW” EMU
bio: The LB&SCR (Later SR) “Coulsden & Wallington” EMUs were a rather more uniform set of vehicles than their previous units, and included a large dedicated luggage vehicle in the centre of the formation. They were soon converted to standard 3rd rail power supply.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The LB&SCR (Later SR) “Coulsden & Wallington” EMUs were a rather more uniform set of vehicles than their previous units, and included a large dedicated luggage vehicle in the centre of the formation. name: LB&SCR “Milk Van” MLV
bio: The motor luggage vans powering the CW EMUs were usable locomotives in their own right, and could operate independantly if needed.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The motor luggage vans powering the CW EMUs were usable locomotives in their own right, and could operate independantly if needed.

name: LNWR Electric Unit
bio: The LNWR built electric multiple units for its inner suburban London routes, sharing track with the Bakerloo and District lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The LNWR built electric multiple units for its inner suburban London routes, sharing track with the Bakerloo and District lines.

name: Southern Railway 5-BEL
bio: The Southern Railway's "Brighton Belle" used Pullman coaches and remained in use well into the BR era.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}40% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Southern Railway's "Brighton Belle" used Pullman coaches and remained in use well into the BR era.

name: Southern Railway 4-COR
bio: The Southern Railway was an early pioneer of electrification, with 4-COR units used between London Waterloo and Portsmouth.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The Southern Railway was an early pioneer of electrification, with 4-COR units used between London Waterloo and Portsmouth.

name: Southern Railway 2-HAL
bio: The 2-HAL (named for 2 units, Half Lavatory) is designed for suburban commuter services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The 2-HAL (named for 2 units, Half Lavatory) is designed for suburban commuter services.

name: SR 4DD Double Decker
bio: SR's 4DD was an experiment to see if double deck multiple units could increase capacity. The internal layout was compromised by the British loading gauge, and it was slow to load at stations.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}SR's 4DD was an experiment to see if double deck multiple units could increase capacity. The internal layout was compromised by the British loading gauge, and it was slow to load at stations.

name: BR Class 506
bio: The Class 506 was built by Metro Cammell and BRCW for the Woodhead Line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.1 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 506 was built by Metro Cammell and BRCW for the Woodhead Line.

name: Cravens Class 105
bio: The Class 105 had a long and hard life on British Railways, mostly around the Eastern Region. Sadly this meant few survived in a viable condition for preservation.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 105 had a long and hard life on British Railways, mostly around the Eastern Region. Sadly this meant few survived in a viable condition for preservation.

name: BR Class 201 “Thumper” “4S”
bio: Class 201 ‘Hastings’ Units were constructed to a narrow loading gauge to work through a number of limited clearance tunnels in the southern region. Southern DEMUs of the time had their engines mounted above the frames.

name: BR Class 205 “Thumper” 3H
bio: Class 205 units were a relatively numerous class of high-density non-corridor multiple units that worked services across Hampshire. In many ways they were the classic ‘Thumper’ unit.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Class 205 units were a relatively numerous class of high-density non-corridor multiple units that worked services across Hampshire. In many ways they were the classic ‘Thumper’ unit.

bio: Class 205 units were a relatively numerous class of high-density non-corridor multiple units that worked services across Hampshire. In many ways they were the classic ‘Thumper’ unit.

name: BR Class 414 "2 HAP"
bio: The Class 414 2 HAP (Half Lavatory, electro-Pneumatic brake) is a Southern Region electric multiple unit, geared for express duties.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 414 2 HAP (Half Lavatory, electro-Pneumatic brake) is a Southern Region electric multiple unit, geared for express duties.

name: BR Class 411 “4CEP”
bio: The archetypal Mk1 Express EMU – 4CEP units were an evolution of the earlier southern 4COR units. 133 were built and they enjoyed a long career into the early 2000s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The archetypal Mk1 Express EMU – 4CEP units were an evolution of the earlier southern 4COR units. 133 were built and they enjoyed a long career into the early 2000s.

name: GRC&W Class 128 Parcel Van
bio: The Class 128 was built specifically for carrying parcels by the Gloucester Railway Carriage and Wagon Company.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 128 was built specifically for carrying parcels by the Gloucester Railway Carriage and Wagon Company.

name: Pressed Steel Class 117
bio: The Class 117 was built under licence by Pressed Steel, being a variant of the Class 116 built by BRCW Derby.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.1 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 117 was built under licence by Pressed Steel, being a variant of the Class 116 built by BRCW Derby.

name: BR Class 419 “MLV”
bio: The Class 419 Motor Luggage Van was used to extend the luggage capacity of express services and was capable of running on it’s own batteries within non-electrified areas.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 419 Motor Luggage Van was used to extend the luggage capacity of express services and was capable of running on it’s own batteries within non-electrified areas.

name: BR Class 489 “GLV”
bio: A late conversion of the 2HAP driving motor to a cab and luggage car for Gatwick Express services
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}A late conversion of the 2HAP driving motor to a cab and luggage car for Gatwick Express services

name: BR Class 302 “AM2”
bio: A suburban Mk1 EMU that mainly operated out of East London on the LT&S line. Here we represent the refurbished condition of these units, which lasted until 1998.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}A suburban Mk1 EMU that mainly operated out of East London on the LT&S line. Here we represent the refurbished condition of these units, which lasted until 1998.

name: BR Class 302/9
bio: A later conversion of the Class 302 to a parcels unit involved the addition of roller doors to the bodyside for faster loading and unloading. The prototypes were also painted in a striking Royal Mail red livery.

name: MC Class 251 "Blue Pullman"
bio: The Blue Pullman is an express diesel multiple unit, designed to tempt motorists from their cars with a luxurious inter-city service. This is the 6-car “Midland Region” layout.

name: MC Class 251 "Blue Pullman"
bio: The Blue Pullman is an express diesel multiple unit, designed to tempt motorists from their cars with a luxurious inter-city service. This is the 8-car “Western Region” set layout.

name: Pressed Steel Class 121 "Bubble Car"
bio: The "Bubble Car" units saw a long life on branch lines and rural railways. Two six-cylinder AEC or Leyland engines of 150hp each drive the wheels through a mechanical transmission.

name: BR Class 309/1 “Clacton Express”
bio: The ‘Clacton’ units were iconic express EMUs of the GEML’s early electrification. This subclass represents a 2 car formation, used to boost 2nd class capacity and power on peak time services.

name: BR Class 309/2 “Clacton Express”
bio: The ‘Clacton’ units were iconic express EMUs of the GEML’s early electrification. This subclass represents a 4 car formation complete with the famous ‘Griddle Car’ restaurant.

name: BREL Class 421 "4 CIG"
bio: The Class 421 4 CIG (Corridor, brIGhton) was a third rail EMU with express gearing, used in the Southern Region. It was based on the Mk1 carriage design.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 421 4 CIG (Corridor, brIGhton) was a third rail EMU with express gearing, used in the Southern Region. It was based on the Mk1 carriage design.

name: BR Class 310 AM10
bio: The AM10 was the first EMU to be based on the Mk2 carriage bodyshell. They were introduced as part of the West Coast Main Line electrification.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The AM10 was the first EMU to be based on the Mk2 carriage bodyshell. They were introduced as part of the West Coast Main Line electrification.

name: Tyne and Wear Metrocar
bio: Tyne and Wear’s ‘Metrocars’ were built by Metro-Cammell to serve the Newcastle metro system in the northeast of England – an amalgamation of a number of suburban lines and newly built tunnels.

name: BREL Class 313
bio: The Class 313 is a dual voltage EMU capable of running both on overhead electrified lines and third rail systems.

bio: The Class 210 was an early design for the modernization of DMU services that became the Sprinter series. A diesel-electric design to a very high specification, but through gangways and above-solebar engines like the classic ‘Thumper’. This unit represents 210 001, the 4-car test unit.

bio: The Sprinter replaced first-generation DMUs. Like other second-generation units it was based on the Mark 3 bodyshell. The name refers to its faster acceleration compared to the units it replaced.

name: Bombardier Class 378 "Capitalstar"
bio: The Capitalstar is an electric multiple unit designed specifically for the needs of the London Overground, with longitudinal seating, large amounts of standing room and wide doors for fast loading and unloading.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Capitalstar is an electric multiple unit designed specifically for the needs of the London Overground, with longitudinal seating, large amounts of standing room and wide doors for fast loading and unloading.

name: Siemens Class 374 ‘Velaro D’
bio: Eurostar’s 2010s replacement for the Class 373 units, which were by this point corroding and becoming rather unreliable took a number of years to enter service but allowed Eurostar to begin service to new destinations in Europe.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}16{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}14.2 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}Eurostar’s 2010s replacement for the Class 373 units, which were by this point corroding and becoming rather unreliable took a number of years to enter service but allowed Eurostar to begin service to new destinations in Europe.

name: Siemens Class 374 ‘Velaro D’
bio: A fictional shorter Class 374 variant…
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}12{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}10.7 tiles{}{SILVER}A fictional shorter Class 374 variant…

name: CAF Class 195 "Civity"
bio: The Class 195 is designed to replace Sprinter and Pacer units with a multiple unit of far higher quality and passenger comfort.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 195 is designed to replace Sprinter and Pacer units with a multiple unit of far higher quality and passenger comfort.

name: Stadler Class 755 "FLIRT"
bio: The Class 755 was designed to modernise services on the East Anglia franchise. The design features a distinctive "power pack" containing the diesel engines used for bi-mode operation.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 755 was designed to modernise services on the East Anglia franchise. The design features a distinctive "power pack" containing the diesel engines used for bi-mode operation.

name: Stadler Class 745 "FLIRT"
bio: Stadler’s Class 745 units replaced Class 90 hauled trains on the GEML. The semi-articulated design was required to meet crashworthiness specifications.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}12{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}9.0 tiles{}{SILVER}Stadler’s Class 745 units replaced Class 90 hauled trains on the GEML. The semi-articulated design was required to meet crashworthiness specifications.

name: Hitachi Class 385 "AT200"
bio: The Class 385 was built for use on newly electrified lines in Scotland. Unlike most AT200 variants, it has cab end doors to allow for trolley services throughout the train.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 385 was built for use on newly electrified lines in Scotland. Unlike most AT200 variants, it has cab end doors to allow for trolley services throughout the train.

name: Siemens Class 700 "Desiro City"
bio: The Class 700 was one of multiple designs evaluated for the Thameslink programme. Although one of the more conventional designs, it offered good capacity and passenger comfort with a relatively low axle load.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}8{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}6.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 700 was one of multiple designs evaluated for the Thameslink programme. Although one of the more conventional designs, it offered good capacity and passenger comfort with a relatively low axle load.

name: Siemens Class 707 "Desiro City"
bio: South West Trains procured the Class 707 as part of capacity improvements for routes out of London Waterloo.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.8 tiles{}{SILVER}South West Trains procured the Class 707 as part of capacity improvements for routes out of London Waterloo.

name: Siemens Class 717 "Desiro City"
bio: The Class 717 ‘Desiro City’ units are variants of the Class 700 Thameslink units, with front emergency exit doors to allow them to work the Northern City Line. They replaced earlier PEP units on King’s Cross suburban services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}6{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 717 ‘Desiro City’ units are variants of the Class 700 Thameslink units, with front emergency exit doors to allow them to work the Northern City Line. They replaced earlier PEP units on King’s Cross suburban services.

name: Bombardier Class 710 "Aventra"
bio: The Class 710 replaces a variety of DMUs and second generation EMUs on London Overground's suburban services. Although there were numerous delays in testing, they finally started entering service in late 2019.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.3 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Class 710 replaces a variety of DMUs and second generation EMUs on London Overground's suburban services. Although there were numerous delays in testing, they finally started entering service in late 2019.

name: Bombardier Class 345 "Aventra"
bio: Designed for London's Crossrail route, the Class 345 entered service on TfL Rail between Liverpool Street and Shenfield.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}9{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}7.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Designed for London's Crossrail route, the Class 345 entered service on TfL Rail between Liverpool Street and Shenfield.

name: Stadler Class 777 "METRO"
bio: The Class 777 "METRO" is a dual-mode unit which charges a battery when on third rail power, which can be used to travel over unpowered track.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 777 "METRO" is a dual-mode unit which charges a battery when on third rail power, which can be used to travel over unpowered track.

name: Bombardier Class 730/2 "Aventra"
bio: The 730/2 is the longer and faster variant of the Class 730 units built for West Midlands Trains, serving on their London Northwestern Railway routes over the West Coast Main Line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.1 tiles{}{SILVER}The 730/2 is the longer and faster variant of the Class 730 units built for West Midlands Trains, serving on their London Northwestern Railway routes over the West Coast Main Line.

name: Hitachi Class 800/0 "Azuma"
bio: The Class 800 "Azuma" (on LNER) or "IET" (on GWR) is a high speed bi-mode unit based on the Hitachi AT300 series. It is designed to replace HST and Class 91 hauled services on Intercity routes.

name: Hitachi Class 801/1 "Azuma"
bio: The Class 801 "Azuma" is LNER’s primary Electric Multiple Unit, procured to replace the Class 91s. It has a single diesel engine for use within depots and during emergencies, but is normally restricted to electrified areas.

name: Hitachi Class 802/0 "IET"
bio: The Class 802 "IET" is a high speed bi-mode unit based on the Hitachi AT300 series. Compared to the Class 800, they have uprated engines and larger fuel tanks, to allow them to operate more easily in non-electrified areas such as Cornwall.

name: Hitachi Class 810 "Aurora"
bio: The Class 810 "Aurora" is a variant on the class 800 series, built to a shorter vehicle length to suit station lengths on the Midland Mainline. It finally began to displace Class 222 DEMUs at the end of 2025.
