It finally began to displace Class 222 DEMUs at the end of 2025.

name: Windhoff MPV (Single)
bio: The Windhoff MPV units were mainly used for Departmental service, notably in the iconic Rail Head Treatment trains, but trials were made in freight service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Windhoff MPV units were mainly used for Departmental service, notably in the iconic Rail Head Treatment trains, but trials were made in freight service.

name: Mather & Platt C&SLR Locomotive
bio: As Tube tunnels started being built at deeper levels, cutting ventilation shafts to use steam locomotives became impractical. To solve this, the City & South London Railway used some of the very first electric locomotives to be built on their lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}As Tube tunnels started being built at deeper levels, cutting ventilation shafts to use steam locomotives became impractical. To solve this, the City & South London Railway used some of the very first electric locomotives to be built on their lines.

name: LU Gate Stock
bio: The Gate Stock was a deep level unit used on Charles Yerkes' Tube lines. It was named for the manual entry gates, each operated by a gateman.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Gate Stock was a deep level unit used on Charles Yerkes' Tube lines. It was named for the manual entry gates, each operated by a gateman.

name: LU Standard Stock
bio: Standard Stock was built to replace Gate stock using modern air-operated doors to improve loading efficiency. At the end of their service with London Underground the units were transferred to the Isle of Wight, continuing to operate until 1991.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Standard Stock was built to replace Gate stock using modern air-operated doors to improve loading efficiency. At the end of their service with London Underground the units were transferred to the Isle of Wight, continuing to operate until 1991.

name: LU O/P Stock
bio: O and P stock were built to similar designs for the Hammersmith&City and Metropolitan lines respectively. They had a long life span, finally being displaced by the C stock's second generation of C77 trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}O and P stock were built to similar designs for the Hammersmith&City and Metropolitan lines respectively. They had a long life span, finally being displaced by the C stock's second generation of C77 trains.

name: LU 1900/1903 Stock
bio: The carriages of the early Central London Railway stock were hauled by locomotives, but these caused excessive vibration so were replaced by motor cars at each end of the train.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}6{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The carriages of the early Central London Railway stock were hauled by locomotives, but these caused excessive vibration so were replaced by motor cars at each end of the train.

name: LU 1938 Stock
bio: The 1938 stock was the first Tube design with all electrical equipment stored under the floor, giving the full length of the car for passenger space. After being retired from the Underground, they were transferred to the Isle of Wight.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}7{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.0 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The 1938 stock was the first Tube design with all electrical equipment stored under the floor, giving the full length of the car for passenger space. After being retired from the Underground, they were transferred to the Isle of Wight.

name: LU 1959 Stock
bio: The 1959 stock was based on the aluminium-bodied 1956 stock, built for the Piccadilly line but used across many deep level Tube lines over its long life.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}7{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.0 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The 1959 stock was based on the aluminium-bodied 1956 stock, built for the Piccadilly line but used across many deep level Tube lines over its long life.

name: LU A60 Stock
bio: The A60 stock replaced T stock and earlier locomotive-hauled services on the Metropolitan line. In original configuration it is the fastest Tube stock built, with a top speed of 70mph.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.3 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The A60 stock replaced T stock and earlier locomotive-hauled services on the Metropolitan line. In original configuration it is the fastest Tube stock built, with a top speed of 70mph.

name: LU 1967 Stock
bio: Introduced for the then-new Victoria line, the 1967 stock featured automatic train operation using coded signals transmitted via the running rails and picked up by coils on the train. The original system struggled to stop in the correct place at stations, and was upgraded with braking profiles recorded from real drivers.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.3 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Introduced for the then-new Victoria line, the 1967 stock featured automatic train operation using coded signals transmitted via the running rails and picked up by coils on the train. The original system struggled to stop in the correct place at stations, and was upgraded with braking profiles recorded from real drivers.

name: LU C69 Stock
bio: The C69 stock is a larger sub-surface unit, used on the District, Circle, and Hammersmith&City lines where 3 units were coupled to form a 6-car train. Despite the name, late trials meant it wasn't introduced until 1970.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.2 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The C69 stock is a larger sub-surface unit, used on the District, Circle, and Hammersmith&City lines where 3 units were coupled to form a 6-car train. Despite the name, late trials meant it wasn't introduced until 1970.

name: LU 1973 Stock
bio: The 1973 stock was built to replace a mixture of earlier units operating the Piccadilly Line. Most were formed as six-car units, but a single three car unit ran the Aldwych branch shuttle until the closure of Aldwich station in 1994.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The 1973 stock was built to replace a mixture of earlier units operating the Piccadilly Line. Most were formed as six-car units, but a single three car unit ran the Aldwych branch shuttle until the closure of Aldwich station in 1994.

name: LU 1973 Stock (Capacity Bug)
bio: This stock has a bug, it carries no passengers. You should not be able to build this or see it in the build window. It will be removed at the next compatibility-breaking change.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}This stock has a bug, it carries no passengers. You should not be able to build this or see it in the build window. It will be removed at the next compatibility-breaking change.

name: LU D78 Stock
bio: The D78 stock replaced pre-war stock on the District Line. It has the longest car lengths to have been used on the Underground network, at 18.37m for a driving motor car.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.1 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The D78 stock replaced pre-war stock on the District Line. It has the longest car lengths to have been used on the Underground network, at 18.37m for a driving motor car.

name: LU 1992 Stock
bio: The 1992 stock was based on one of three prototype Tube stock trains built in 1986, and is used on both Central and Waterloo&City lines. Although the prototypes recorded many testing miles, the stock has still suffered numerous problems including motors detaching from the train.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The 1992 stock was based on one of three prototype Tube stock trains built in 1986, and is used on both Central and Waterloo&City lines. Although the prototypes recorded many testing miles, the stock has still suffered numerous problems including motors detaching from the train.

name: LU 1995 Stock
bio: Outwardly very similar to the 1996 stock used for the Jubilee Line, the Northern Line's 1995 stock features more advanced traction hardware due to a later design finalisation date.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Outwardly very similar to the 1996 stock used for the Jubilee Line, the Northern Line's 1995 stock features more advanced traction hardware due to a later design finalisation date.

name: LU 2009 Stock
bio: The 2009 stock replaced 1967 stock on the Victoria Line, with greatly increased capacity and performance. With 8-car trains, it is the widest and longest stock used on a deep-level Tube line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The 2009 stock replaced 1967 stock on the Victoria Line, with greatly increased capacity and performance. With 8-car trains, it is the widest and longest stock used on a deep-level Tube line.

name: LU S7 Stock
bio: The S stock is the standard stock for London Underground's surface lines. It replaces several types of stock, some of which had become severely outdated and unreliable by 2010.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}7{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The S stock is the standard stock for London Underground's surface lines. It replaces several types of stock, some of which had become severely outdated and unreliable by 2010.

name: LU S Stock
bio: The S stock is the standard stock for London Underground's surface lines. It replaces several types of stock, some of which had become severely outdated and unreliable by 2010. This is a shortened 6-car version to fit in 4 tiles.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}6{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The S stock is the standard stock for London Underground's surface lines. It replaces several types of stock, some of which had become severely outdated and unreliable by 2010. This is a shortened 6-car version to fit in 4 tiles.

name: LU New Tube for London
bio: The New Tube for London is intended to replace all of London Underground's existing deep-level Tube stock, starting with the 1973 stock on the Piccadilly Line.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}9{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.0 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The New Tube for London is intended to replace all of London Underground's existing deep-level Tube stock, starting with the 1973 stock on the Piccadilly Line.

name: Glasgow Subway Stock
bio: The Glasgow Subway uses a smaller than standard gauge and a 600V DC third rail system, although in-game it runs on the 630V DC fourth rail standard gauge tracks to fit with the other metro units.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Glasgow Subway uses a smaller than standard gauge and a 600V DC third rail system, although in-game it runs on the 630V DC fourth rail standard gauge tracks to fit with the other metro units.

name: Early passenger carriage
bio: The first enclosed passenger carriages were adapted from stagecoach bodies, with wooden frames.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The first enclosed passenger carriages were adapted from stagecoach bodies, with wooden frames.

name: Four-wheel carriage
bio: Railway carriages from the 1850s to 1870s tended to be built on wooden or wrought iron frames of around 20-24ft in length, with oil lighting internally.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Railway carriages from the 1850s to 1870s tended to be built on wooden or wrought iron frames of around 20-24ft in length, with oil lighting internally.

name: Six-wheel carriage
bio: After early experiments for royal saloons, smoother and faster-running six wheel carriages with then-modern fixtures such as gas lighting became common from the 1870s, displacing older four wheeled stock to smaller suburban lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}After early experiments for royal saloons, smoother and faster-running six wheel carriages with then-modern fixtures such as gas lighting became common from the 1870s, displacing older four wheeled stock to smaller suburban lines.

name: "Padded Cell" carriage
bio: Early Tube carriages were nicknamed "Padded Cell" due to their tall cushion-backed seats and small windows.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Early Tube carriages were nicknamed "Padded Cell" due to their tall cushion-backed seats and small windows.

name: Clerestory carriage
bio: Toward the end of the Victorian era, passenger carriages began to be mounted on bogies rather than fixed axles. Many featured clerestory-style roofs.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}Toward the end of the Victorian era, passenger carriages began to be mounted on bogies rather than fixed axles. Many featured clerestory-style roofs.

name: Passenger Carriage
bio: In the early 20th century passenger coaches were stretched to greater lengths. This is a typical LNWR design.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}In the early 20th century passenger coaches were stretched to greater lengths. This is a typical LNWR design.

name: Non-corridor carriage
bio: Non-corridor carriages allow greater seating density, at the cost of passenger comfort.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Non-corridor carriages allow greater seating density, at the cost of passenger comfort.

name: LNWR Sleeping Car
bio: Early 20th century sleeping cars were often heavier and larger than their daytime counterparts. This represents a wooden-bodied, 6-axle sleeping coach of the golden era of train travel, in reality there were many variants built in many batches.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Early 20th century sleeping cars were often heavier and larger than their daytime counterparts. This represents a wooden-bodied, 6-axle sleeping coach of the golden era of train travel, in reality there were many variants built in many batches.

name: Pullman carriage
bio: The luxurious Pullman cars were large and heavy, with many still in use today for restaurant services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}40% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The luxurious Pullman cars were large and heavy, with many still in use today for restaurant services.

name: Streamlined Passenger Carriage
bio: As express services increased in speed, carriages were streamlined with valances between bogies and flexible walls connecting each carriage.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}As express services increased in speed, carriages were streamlined with valances between bogies and flexible walls connecting each carriage.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (TSO)
bio: The Mk1 carriage combined best practice from the different Big Four carriage designs into a single standard design that could be used across the BR network. TSO (Tourist Second/Standard Open) was a common type, with 2+2 seating in an open saloon.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Mk1 carriage combined best practice from the different Big Four carriage designs into a single standard design that could be used across the BR network. TSO (Tourist Second/Standard Open) was a common type, with 2+2 seating in an open saloon.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (FO)
bio: Mk1 FO (First Open) carriages had wider and more luxurious seating in a 2+1 arrangement in an open saloon.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mk1 FO (First Open) carriages had wider and more luxurious seating in a 2+1 arrangement in an open saloon.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (SK)
bio: Mk1 SK (Second/Standard Corridor) carriages were divided into 8 compartments, accessed by a gangway along one side of the carriage. This was the most common of all Mk1 carriage types.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}5% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mk1 SK (Second/Standard Corridor) carriages were divided into 8 compartments, accessed by a gangway along one side of the carriage. This was the most common of all Mk1 carriage types.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (FK)
bio: MK1 FK (First Corridor) carriages were divided into 7 compartments, larger and more luxurious than the SK carriages.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}MK1 FK (First Corridor) carriages were divided into 7 compartments, larger and more luxurious than the SK carriages.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (RMB)
bio: The Mk1 RMB (Restaurant Mini Buffet) is a TSO carriage in which some of the seating rows have been replaced with a small buffet service restaurant.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}The Mk1 RMB (Restaurant Mini Buffet) is a TSO carriage in which some of the seating rows have been replaced with a small buffet service restaurant.

name: BR Mk1 Sleeper
bio: The Mk1 Sleeper was built in relatively large numbers. The type was not especially popular with passengers, but the service was still an improvement on sleeping in ordinary compartment stock of the time.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk1 Sleeper was built in relatively large numbers. The type was not especially popular with passengers, but the service was still an improvement on sleeping in ordinary compartment stock of the time.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (RMB, alternate livery)
bio: The Mk1 RMB (Restaurant Mini Buffet) is a TSO carriage in which some of the seating rows have been replaced with a small buffet service restaurant. This version is in an alternate livery to match the corridor carriages.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}The Mk1 RMB (Restaurant Mini Buffet) is a TSO carriage in which some of the seating rows have been replaced with a small buffet service restaurant. This version is in an alternate livery to match the corridor carriages.

name: BR Mk1 suburban carriage
bio: BR built many suburban Mk1 carriages with no gangways, although typically only one or two would be used per train across most of the country.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}BR built many suburban Mk1 carriages with no gangways, although typically only one or two would be used per train across most of the country.

name: BR Mk2 carriage (TSO)
bio: The Mk2 carriage is the second BR carriage design. It was built to be stronger than the Mk1, giving better corrosion resistance and being safer for passengers in an accident.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Mk2 carriage is the second BR carriage design. It was built to be stronger than the Mk1, giving better corrosion resistance and being safer for passengers in an accident.

name: BR Mk2 carriage (FO)
bio: Mk2 FO (First Open) carriages followed the Mk1 approach of having wider, more luxurious seating in an open saloon. BR preferred open interiors by this point, although a few corridor Mk2s were still built early on.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mk2 FO (First Open) carriages followed the Mk1 approach of having wider, more luxurious seating in an open saloon. BR preferred open interiors by this point, although a few corridor Mk2s were still built early on.

name: BR Mk2 carriage (FK)
bio: A few Mk2 FK (First Corridor) carriages were built early in the model's life, featuring a similar 7 compartment design to the Mk1. Although BR did not build any SK carriages initially, later a few Mk2 FKs were adapted to standard class.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}A few Mk2 FK (First Corridor) carriages were built early in the model's life, featuring a similar 7 compartment design to the Mk1. Although BR did not build any SK carriages initially, later a few Mk2 FKs were adapted to standard class.

name: BR Mk2F carriage (TSO)
bio: The Mark 2F was the final development of the Mark 2 carriage, introducing many features which would be used on the Mark 3 Intercity carriages.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Mark 2F was the final development of the Mark 2 carriage, introducing many features which would be used on the Mark 3 Intercity carriages.

name: BR Mk2F carriage (FO)
bio: By the point of the Mk2F, BR had abandoned corridor carriage layouts, using only open saloons even for first class.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}By the point of the Mk2F, BR had abandoned corridor carriage layouts, using only open saloons even for first class.

name: BR Mk2F carriage (RFB)
bio: After the Mark 1's bewildering array of restaurant car types, BR built very few Mark 2 dining cars, all during the final Mk2f iteration. The RFB (Restaurant First Class Buffet) is a variant with first-class seating.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}After the Mark 1's bewildering array of restaurant car types, BR built very few Mark 2 dining cars, all during the final Mk2f iteration. The RFB (Restaurant First Class Buffet) is a variant with first-class seating.

name: BR Mk2F carriage (BG)
bio: By the 1970s BR had ceased to build carriages with the intention of adding parcel vans to passenger services, but that hasn't stopped railfans and modellers wondering what could have been.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}By the 1970s BR had ceased to build carriages with the intention of adding parcel vans to passenger services, but that hasn't stopped railfans and modellers wondering what could have been.{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: BR Mk3 carriage (TS/TSO)
bio: The Mk3 carriage is designed for higher speed operation, capable of running at up to 125mph. The first Mk3 carriages were built for the prototype HST. Loco-hauled Mk3a carriages used the TSO designation, whereas those in an HST rake had different electrical equipment and were designated TS (Trailer Standard) as a full HST set is considered a DMU for classification purposes.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk3 carriage is designed for higher speed operation, capable of running at up to 125mph. The first Mk3 carriages were built for the prototype HST. Loco-hauled Mk3a carriages used the TSO designation, whereas those in an HST rake had different electrical equipment and were designated TS (Trailer Standard) as a full HST set is considered a DMU for classification purposes.

name: BR Mk3 carriage (FO)
bio: Mk3 carriages followed the by now standard practice of first class vehicles featuring an open saloon with lower seating density than standard class.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}30% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mk3 carriages followed the by now standard practice of first class vehicles featuring an open saloon with lower seating density than standard class.

name: BR Mk3 carriage (RUB)
bio: Although HST sets had their own catering vehicles, BR continued to use Mk1 restaurant vehicles even with newer coaching stock for loco-hauled services. In 1979 the first Mk3 loco-hauled buffet cars finally arrived, with the RUB (Restaurant Unclassified Buffet) featuring an unclassified seating area.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}Although HST sets had their own catering vehicles, BR continued to use Mk1 restaurant vehicles even with newer coaching stock for loco-hauled services. In 1979 the first Mk3 loco-hauled buffet cars finally arrived, with the RUB (Restaurant Unclassified Buffet) featuring an unclassified seating area.

name: BR Mk3 carriage (RFM)
bio: The limited offerings of BR's buffet cars were a staple of many '70s and '80s stand up comedy routines. BR responded to increasing passenger expectations in the mid 1980s by converting older Mk3 vehicles to RFM (Restaurant First Modular) specification, featuring a kitchen area and seating around tables.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}The limited offerings of BR's buffet cars were a staple of many '70s and '80s stand up comedy routines. BR responded to increasing passenger expectations in the mid 1980s by converting older Mk3 vehicles to RFM (Restaurant First Modular) specification, featuring a kitchen area and seating around tables.

name: BR Mk3 Sleeper
bio: The Mk3 Sleeper was a significant upgrade over the previous stock – being air conditioned and air sprung. Two berths per cabin unfortunately give these vehicles a low capacity. This represents a typical Sleeper, Either Class, Pantry (SLEP) vehicle.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk3 Sleeper was a significant upgrade over the previous stock – being air conditioned and air sprung. Two berths per cabin unfortunately give these vehicles a low capacity. This represents a typical Sleeper, Either Class, Pantry (SLEP) vehicle.

name: BR Mk3 carriage (GUV)
bio: BR did not build any carriage-based utility vans after the Mk1, but this vehicle is included as a "what could have been" if HSTs and express services had included a high speed parcels car.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}BR did not build any carriage-based utility vans after the Mk1, but this vehicle is included as a "what could have been" if HSTs and express services had included a high speed parcels car.{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: BREL "APT-S" Trailer Rake (10 cars)
bio: An articulated trailer rake of APT-S Vehicles, intended to run with a Driving Motor and a DVT but also usable in other weird combinations.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}10{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}7.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}An articulated trailer rake of APT-S Vehicles, intended to run with a Driving Motor and a DVT but also usable in other weird combinations.

name: BREL "APT-S" Trailer Rake (6 cars)
bio: An articulated trailer rake of APT-S Vehicles, intended to run with a Driving Motor and a DVT but also usable in other weird combinations.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}6{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}An articulated trailer rake of APT-S Vehicles, intended to run with a Driving Motor and a DVT but also usable in other weird combinations.

name: BR Mk4 carriage (TSO)
bio: The Mk4 carriage is a further improvement on the Mk3. It's designed for use with the Class 91 for Intercity 225 services. Access is improved with greater consideration for wheelchair users.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk4 carriage is a further improvement on the Mk3. It's designed for use with the Class 91 for Intercity 225 services. Access is improved with greater consideration for wheelchair users.

name: BR Mk4 carriage (FO)
bio: The Mk4 carriage is a further improvement on the Mk3. It's designed for use with the Class 91 for Intercity 225 services. A few FO carriages were converted to FOS (for smoking) until smoking was banned on all british train services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}35% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk4 carriage is a further improvement on the Mk3. It's designed for use with the Class 91 for Intercity 225 services. A few FO carriages were converted to FOS (for smoking) until smoking was banned on all british train services.

name: BR Mk4 carriage (RFB)
bio: Intercity 225 formations included restaurant cars from their introduction, initially as first class RFB cars.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}Intercity 225 formations included restaurant cars from their introduction, initially as first class RFB cars.

name: BR Mk4 carriage (RSB)
bio: As LNER took over East Coast Express services, RSB cars with standard-class seating were introduced, allowing more passengers to sit in the buffet car.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}As LNER took over East Coast Express services, RSB cars with standard-class seating were introduced, allowing more passengers to sit in the buffet car.

name: BR Mk4 carriage (GUV)
bio: By the time the Mk4 was introduced, the end of post on the rail was in sight, with Travelling Post Office services ceasing in 1996. But what if BR had continued to build parcel vans?
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}By the time the Mk4 was introduced, the end of post on the rail was in sight, with Travelling Post Office services ceasing in 1996. But what if BR had continued to build parcel vans?{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: EPS “Nightstar” Sleeper (7 Car)
bio: The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. The 7-car sets were intended for service to the West Country, Wales and Scotland.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}7{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}5.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}{SILVER}The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. The 7-car sets were intended for service to the West Country, Wales and Scotland.

name: EPS “Nightstar” Sleeper (8 Car)
bio: The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. 8-car sets were intended for flagship service between London and major European cities.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}8{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}6.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}{SILVER}The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. 8-car sets were intended for flagship service between London and major European cities.

name: EPS “Nightstar” Generator Car
bio: The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.

name: EPS “Nightstar” Generator Car (Alternate Livery)
bio: The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.

name: CAF Mk5A carriage
bio: The Mark 5A was built for loco-hauled services on the TransPennine Express, but was withdrawn barely four years after introduction.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mark 5A was built for loco-hauled services on the TransPennine Express, but was withdrawn barely four years after introduction.

name: CAF Mk5 Sleeper (Berths)
bio: The Mk5 sleeper trains were procured to replace Mk3 sleepers. With the end of double berths, these trains have an even lower capacity than what they replaced!
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk5 sleeper trains were procured to replace Mk3 sleepers. With the end of double berths, these trains have an even lower capacity than what they replaced!

name: CAF Mk5 Sleeper (Seated)
bio: Mk5 sleeper services make up for their pathetic berth count with conventional seated stock, featuring a 2+1 airline style seating and not enough recline in the seats.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mk5 sleeper services make up for their pathetic berth count with conventional seated stock, featuring a 2+1 airline style seating and not enough recline in the seats.

name: CAF Mk5 Sleeper (Club Car)
bio: The Mk5 ‘Club Car’ is the dining facility for sleeper services, but the 30 seats within are not bookable for travel.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}The Mk5 ‘Club Car’ is the dining facility for sleeper services, but the 30 seats within are not bookable for travel.

name: BR Mk1 BG (Prototype)
bio: The standard parcels and baggage car on British Rail for decades, the Mk1 Brake Gangwayed was extremely versatile. This represents a standard 57’ frame example, making it noticably shorter than the standard Mk1 coach.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The standard parcels and baggage car on British Rail for decades, the Mk1 Brake Gangwayed was extremely versatile. This represents a standard 57’ frame example, making it noticably shorter than the standard Mk1 coach.

name: BR Mk1 BG (B4 Bogies)
bio: In the 1980s a number of BG vehicles were upgraded with B4 bogies and cleared for travel at 110mph, to provide additional capacity within fast passenger services, which were typically run with Mark 2 stock.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}In the 1980s a number of BG vehicles were upgraded with B4 bogies and cleared for travel at 110mph, to provide additional capacity within fast passenger services, which were typically run with Mark 2 stock.

name: BR Mk1 Super BG
bio: By the 90s parcels traffic was in decline on the railway, and increasingly movement focused on fixed flows between mail terminals. As a result, a number of vehicles were rebuilt with roller shutter doors for faster loading.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}By the 90s parcels traffic was in decline on the railway, and increasingly movement focused on fixed flows between mail terminals. As a result, a number of vehicles were rebuilt with roller shutter doors for faster loading.

name: BR FVX Carflat
bio: The ‘Carflat’ Wagons were simply coach underframes with wooden decks and low sides added, normally used to convey assembled vehicles. In many cases, these were used in passenger train formations, in the Motorail service as part of sleeper trains.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}{SILVER}The ‘Carflat’ Wagons were simply coach underframes with wooden decks and low sides added, normally used to convey assembled vehicles. In many cases, these were used in passenger train formations, in the Motorail service as part of sleeper trains.

name: BR XMA Cartic-4
bio: Large and articulated, the cartic 4 sets were built for motorail services and used to move new cars from factories to export.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}{SILVER}Large and articulated, the cartic 4 sets were built for motorail services and used to move new cars from factories to export.

name: CIWL Type F Sleeping Car
bio: The Franco-Belgian Compagnie Internationale des Wagons-Lits ran a private sleeper service from London, via the Southern Railway and a cross-channel ferry, to connect to the Orient Express in Paris. A fleet of 25 all-steel sleeping cars was built for the service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Franco-Belgian Compagnie Internationale des Wagons-Lits ran a private sleeper service from London, via the Southern Railway and a cross-channel ferry, to connect to the Orient Express in Paris. A fleet of 25 all-steel sleeping cars was built for the service.

name: CIWL Fourgon Baggage Car
bio: These two-axle baggage cars were a fixture of the night ferry service, running with the sleeping cars on the 11 hour schedule from London to Paris.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}These two-axle baggage cars were a fixture of the night ferry service, running with the sleeping cars on the 11 hour schedule from London to Paris.

name: Early brake carriage
bio: Although dedicated brake carriages were not common until the 1840s, this one is included in the set to allow early parcel and mail transport.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Although dedicated brake carriages were not common until the 1840s, this one is included in the set to allow early parcel and mail transport.

name: Four-wheel brake carriage
bio: Early four-wheel carriages did not have locomotive-controlled brakes so, like freight trains, were reliant on the brakes in the guard's van.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Early four-wheel carriages did not have locomotive-controlled brakes so, like freight trains, were reliant on the brakes in the guard's van.

name: Six-wheel brake carriage
bio: Although carriages gained more luxurious fixtures and fittings through the Victorian era, braking still required a guard's van.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Although carriages gained more luxurious fixtures and fittings through the Victorian era, braking still required a guard's van.

name: Clerestory brake carriage
bio: This brake carriage is designed for use with clerestory carriages. It can carry parcels and mail.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}This brake carriage is designed for use with clerestory carriages. It can carry parcels and mail.

name: Brake carriage
bio: Brake carriages continued to be a fixture of passenger trains during the steam era, carrying bulky luggage and parcels.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Brake carriages continued to be a fixture of passenger trains during the steam era, carrying bulky luggage and parcels.

name: BR Mk1 carriage (BG Conversion)
bio: BG for (Brake Gangway) is the BR designation for a brake coach. As well as a guard's compartment it had two large areas for storing luggage.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}BG for (Brake Gangway) is the BR designation for a brake coach. As well as a guard's compartment it had two large areas for storing luggage.{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: BR Mk2 carriage (BG)
bio: BR started rationalising the number of carriage variants with the Mk2 so there was no dedicated parcels van, and most consists used a Mk1 BG attached to Mk2 passenger stock.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}BR started rationalising the number of carriage variants with the Mk2 so there was no dedicated parcels van, and most consists used a Mk1 BG attached to Mk2 passenger stock.{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: BR TPO carriage
bio: Based on the Mark 1 carriage, the TPO (Travelling Post Office) allowed mail to be sorted as it was transported.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Based on the Mark 1 carriage, the TPO (Travelling Post Office) allowed mail to be sorted as it was transported.

name: Open wagon
bio: Early open wagons had simple four-wheeled structures and were typically pulled by horses on quarry lines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Early open wagons had simple four-wheeled structures and were typically pulled by horses on quarry lines.

name: 3-plank open wagon
bio: Open "plank" wagons are named for the number of planks used in the construction of their sides.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Open "plank" wagons are named for the number of planks used in the construction of their sides.

name: 5-plank open wagon
bio: The 5 plank wagon became one of the common standard types used by the GWR, with thousands built.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The 5 plank wagon became one of the common standard types used by the GWR, with thousands built.

name: Steel open wagon
bio: Steel wagons were introduced in the 1930s to have a longer service life than wooden wagons, which were proving uneconomical for many owners to operate after recessions at the start of the decade.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Steel wagons were introduced in the 1930s to have a longer service life than wooden wagons, which were proving uneconomical for many owners to operate after recessions at the start of the decade.

name: LNER hopper
bio: The LNER built many of these coal hoppers, from 1935 to nationalisation. Although capacity is lower, these can be loaded faster than regular wagons.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The LNER built many of these coal hoppers, from 1935 to nationalisation. Although capacity is lower, these can be loaded faster than regular wagons.

name: OHV open wagon
bio: BR continued to make steel bodied wagons following LNER designs, gradually altering the body style.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}BR continued to make steel bodied wagons following LNER designs, gradually altering the body style.

name: OAA open wagon
bio: The OAA/OBA wagons were BR's first air braked open wagons, later forming part of the Speedlink network. They featured steel end frames with wooden drop sides.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The OAA/OBA wagons were BR's first air braked open wagons, later forming part of the Speedlink network. They featured steel end frames with wooden drop sides.

name: MEA open wagon
bio: MEA wagons used frames from old hopper wagons combined with a new box body.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}MEA wagons used frames from old hopper wagons combined with a new box body.

name: MWA open wagon
bio: The MWA is another wagon rebuilt using new bodies on an old frame, resulting in a high capacity bogie open wagon.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The MWA is another wagon rebuilt using new bodies on an old frame, resulting in a high capacity bogie open wagon.

name: MGR hopper
bio: MGR "merry-go-round" hoppers enabled coal trains to load and unload without stopping, emptying coal into discharge hoppers underneath the wagons. In OpenTTD this is represented by faster loading speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}MGR "merry-go-round" hoppers enabled coal trains to load and unload without stopping, emptying coal into discharge hoppers underneath the wagons. In OpenTTD this is represented by faster loading speeds.

name: HHA hopper
bio: The HHA is a large self-discharging hopper built for Freightliner to use on coal trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The HHA is a large self-discharging hopper built for Freightliner to use on coal trains.

name: Conflat container wagon
bio: Conflats were built to carry containers, meaning goods could be loaded separately and then the whole container placed on the train in one operation.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Conflats were built to carry containers, meaning goods could be loaded separately and then the whole container placed on the train in one operation.

name: FFA container wagon
bio: The FFA container wagons were a major advance over short-wheelbase conflats when built, and being easily adapted to the then-new ISO container standard only increased this advantage.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The FFA container wagons were a major advance over short-wheelbase conflats when built, and being easily adapted to the then-new ISO container standard only increased this advantage.

name: FSA container wagon
bio: Freightliner had considered replacing the FFA wagons with a shorter 40ft container flat, but the poor running quality of fixed axle wagons resulted in the choice of another standard bogie flat.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Freightliner had considered replacing the FFA wagons with a shorter 40ft container flat, but the poor running quality of fixed axle wagons resulted in the choice of another standard bogie flat.

name: KTA "Pocket" wagon
bio: KTA wagons solve the problem of carrying high-cube containers in the small British loading gauge, using a "pocket" between the axles that can take either a single 40ft container or two 20ft ones.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}KTA wagons solve the problem of carrying high-cube containers in the small British loading gauge, using a "pocket" between the axles that can take either a single 40ft container or two 20ft ones.

name: Flat wagon
bio: Early flat wagons had simple wooden construction, and sometimes featured stakes for securing loads.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Early flat wagons had simple wooden construction, and sometimes featured stakes for securing loads.

name: Bolster wagon
bio: Flat wagons carried goods such as metal pipes and wood, where a flat surface with bolsters for tying loads down was most convenient.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Flat wagons carried goods such as metal pipes and wood, where a flat surface with bolsters for tying loads down was most convenient.

name: GWR "Beaver" flat wagon
bio: "Beaver" was the GWR telegraph code for a flat wagon, with an additional letter used to identify the length.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}"Beaver" was the GWR telegraph code for a flat wagon, with an additional letter used to identify the length.

name: Bogie bolster wagon
bio: Bolster wagons were some of the earliest goods vehicles to use bogies rather than fixed axles. The wooden bolsters running across the wagon made long, rigid loads easy to tie down.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}Bolster wagons were some of the earliest goods vehicles to use bogies rather than fixed axles. The wooden bolsters running across the wagon made long, rigid loads easy to tie down.

name: LMS double bolster
bio: The LMS built double bolsters to carry welded rail, with single rails laid across multiple bolster wagons.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The LMS built double bolsters to carry welded rail, with single rails laid across multiple bolster wagons.

name: YMO "Salmon" bolster wagon
bio: The "Salmon" wagons were built in large quantities in the early 1950s, and lasted well into privatisation. Real-life versions suffered bearing problems and were only able to run at 50 or 40mph until being refitted around the turn of the century.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The "Salmon" wagons were built in large quantities in the early 1950s, and lasted well into privatisation. Real-life versions suffered bearing problems and were only able to run at 50 or 40mph until being refitted around the turn of the century.

name: BR "Lowmac"
bio: "Lowmac" was a GWR code for a low wagon intended to fit tall, bulky cargo within the railway loading gauge. BR built a number of small Lowmac types in the early 1950s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}"Lowmac" was a GWR code for a low wagon intended to fit tall, bulky cargo within the railway loading gauge. BR built a number of small Lowmac types in the early 1950s.

name: BDA bolster wagon
bio: In the 1970s BR found itself with a large number of wagons that still had useful service life, but were incompatible with the air braked standard employed across the network. Refurbishing them with air brakes and new bogies produced wagons such as the BDA.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}In the 1970s BR found itself with a large number of wagons that still had useful service life, but were incompatible with the air braked standard employed across the network. Refurbishing them with air brakes and new bogies produced wagons such as the BDA.

name: YKA "Osprey" bolster wagon
bio: Despite the age of the "Salmon" bolster wagons, in 2009 they were refurbished with new end plates to make loading track panels simpler.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}Despite the age of the "Salmon" bolster wagons, in 2009 they were refurbished with new end plates to make loading track panels simpler.

name: FEA-W "Supertench" bolster wagon
bio: The FEA-W is a modular wagon built by Wascosa, based on a strong metal spine which can be topped by a variety of modules for different purposes.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The FEA-W is a modular wagon built by Wascosa, based on a strong metal spine which can be topped by a variety of modules for different purposes.

name: BAA steel wagon
bio: BAA wagons could initially carry steel in a large variety of forms, although many were later modified to be dedicated coil carriers.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}BAA wagons could initially carry steel in a large variety of forms, although many were later modified to be dedicated coil carriers.

name: Cargowaggon IGA Flat Wagon
bio: The basic chassis of the Cargowaggon van adapted naturally to a sturdy, high-capacity flat wagon, used for steel, wood and other cargo, then later adapted for departmental work carrying welded rail and as match wagons.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The basic chassis of the Cargowaggon van adapted naturally to a sturdy, high-capacity flat wagon, used for steel, wood and other cargo, then later adapted for departmental work carrying welded rail and as match wagons.

name: BYA "Nissen Hut" steel wagon
bio: The BYA/BRA covered steel carriers gained their name from the corrugated sliding cover and its curved cross section.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The BYA/BRA covered steel carriers gained their name from the corrugated sliding cover and its curved cross section.

name: Four-wheel tank wagon
bio: Early four wheel tank wagons are limited to low speeds by their construction and primitive suspension.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Early four wheel tank wagons are limited to low speeds by their construction and primitive suspension.

name: Cylindrical tank wagon
bio: The GWR's cylindrical tank wagons were built by the Locomotive department, as the iron cylinders used similar techniques to steam engines.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The GWR's cylindrical tank wagons were built by the Locomotive department, as the iron cylinders used similar techniques to steam engines.

name: Six wheeled tank wagon
bio: Six wheeled tank wagons formed the core of the GWR (and later BR) "milk trains", long freight consists transporting milk from farms to cities.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Six wheeled tank wagons formed the core of the GWR (and later BR) "milk trains", long freight consists transporting milk from farms to cities.

name: TTA tank wagon
bio: TTA tankers were constructed on "monobloc" principles and featured automatic braking.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}TTA tankers were constructed on "monobloc" principles and featured automatic braking.

name: TEA tank wagon
bio: Large bogie tankers allowed for carrying heavy loads of liquids at high speeds. The long frames allow tanks to take up more space without fouling axles.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Large bogie tankers allowed for carrying heavy loads of liquids at high speeds. The long frames allow tanks to take up more space without fouling axles.

name: LMS Grain Hopper
bio: The LMS Grain Hooper was later standardised into the BR COVGRAIN standard design. Long-distance bulk grain service in covered hoppers was a significant efficiency gain over shipping bagged grain by the vanload.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The LMS Grain Hooper was later standardised into the BR COVGRAIN standard design. Long-distance bulk grain service in covered hoppers was a significant efficiency gain over shipping bagged grain by the vanload.

name: BR Grain Hopper
bio: This is a representative BR GRAINFIT wagon, a vacuum-braked bulk hopper derived from the 1930s LMS design.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}This is a representative BR GRAINFIT wagon, a vacuum-braked bulk hopper derived from the 1930s LMS design.

name: “Grainflow” Covered Hopper
bio: The BR ‘Whiskey Covhops’ were a common sight on long-distance freight trains from southern England, headed for the distilleries of Scotland.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The BR ‘Whiskey Covhops’ were a common sight on long-distance freight trains from southern England, headed for the distilleries of Scotland.

name: “Polybulk” Covered Hopper
bio: Large bogie covered hoppers first appeared in Britain in the mid 1970s. These were frequently privately owned and registered as international vehicles. This slab-sided ‘Polybulk’ example was built in France.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Large bogie covered hoppers first appeared in Britain in the mid 1970s. These were frequently privately owned and registered as international vehicles. This slab-sided ‘Polybulk’ example was built in France.

name: Livestock van
bio: Livestock traffic was common early on in railway history, with specialised cattle wagons and stations equipped to handle loading and unloading.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Livestock traffic was common early on in railway history, with specialised cattle wagons and stations equipped to handle loading and unloading.

name: 30ft Livestock van
bio: Livestock vans evolved along with improvements in covered van design.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Livestock vans evolved along with improvements in covered van design.

name: SCV cattle van
bio: Specialised cattle vans such as this Southern Railway example were built from the 1930s to the 1950s, although livestock traffic reduced in the latter part of this period.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Specialised cattle vans such as this Southern Railway example were built from the 1930s to the 1950s, although livestock traffic reduced in the latter part of this period.

name: 35ft livestock van
bio: In reality by the 1960s cattle traffic on the railways had heavily declined with few stations still equipped to handle livestock, and eventually ceased altogether. This wagon represents a plausible development had mainline livestock traffic continued.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}In reality by the 1960s cattle traffic on the railways had heavily declined with few stations still equipped to handle livestock, and eventually ceased altogether. This wagon represents a plausible development had mainline livestock traffic continued.{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: 40ft livestock van
bio: Although there is no modern livestock traffic on railways in the UK, other countries have continued to build livestock wagons. This wagon is representative of contemporary livestock transport, if it were built to British loading gauge.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Although there is no modern livestock traffic on railways in the UK, other countries have continued to build livestock wagons. This wagon is representative of contemporary livestock transport, if it were built to British loading gauge.{}{}{GREEN}Included for gameplay purposes

name: RCH Salt Wagon
bio: The standard RCH Salt Wagon design of 1907 was long-lived in private ownership and saw some use beyond sodium chloride, which was largely used as part of the chemical industry.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The standard RCH Salt Wagon design of 1907 was long-lived in private ownership and saw some use beyond sodium chloride, which was largely used as part of the chemical industry.

name: “Presflo” Powder Hopper
bio: The Presflo covered hopper was designed specifically to carry cement powder, being unloaded via a pressurized air connection and liquefaction of the cargo.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Presflo covered hopper was designed specifically to carry cement powder, being unloaded via a pressurized air connection and liquefaction of the cargo.

name: PCA “Vee” Powder Tanker
bio: The PCA tanker, with it’s notable depressed-center ‘vee’ appearance, could carry large amounts of cement and was a common sight in mixed freight trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The PCA tanker, with it’s notable depressed-center ‘vee’ appearance, could carry large amounts of cement and was a common sight in mixed freight trains.

name: JPA Powder Tanker
bio: By the 2000s the BR-era 2-axle cement tankers then in use were beginning to show their age. They were replaced by these large bogie tank wagons built in Germany, with a distinctive fish-belly shape.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}By the 2000s the BR-era 2-axle cement tankers then in use were beginning to show their age. They were replaced by these large bogie tank wagons built in Germany, with a distinctive fish-belly shape.

name: Covered wagon
bio: Simple canvas-covered wagons were some of the earliest vehicles for moving piece goods which needed to be sheltered from the elements.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Simple canvas-covered wagons were some of the earliest vehicles for moving piece goods which needed to be sheltered from the elements.

name: Wooden-framed van
bio: Piece goods were an important source of revenue for early railways, with most building large quantities of covered vans.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Piece goods were an important source of revenue for early railways, with most building large quantities of covered vans.

name: Iron-framed van
bio: This goods van uses an iron frame with a wooden body, making it stronger than earlier wooden-framed vans.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}This goods van uses an iron frame with a wooden body, making it stronger than earlier wooden-framed vans.

name: Fitted van
bio: Fitted vans had brakes which could be controlled from the locomotive or a brake van, allowing higher braking force and thus speed. This example is typical of a Southern Railways vacuum-braked model.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Fitted vans had brakes which could be controlled from the locomotive or a brake van, allowing higher braking force and thus speed. This example is typical of a Southern Railways vacuum-braked model.

name: VWV "Vanwide" van
bio: The Vanwide was BR's final development of the traditional van design, featuring wider doors for increased loading speed.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Vanwide was BR's final development of the traditional van design, featuring wider doors for increased loading speed.

name: BR VIX Ferry Van
bio: BR’s two-axle Ferry Vans were built for interoperation with european service via cross-channel ferry services, allowing British companies to send goods directly to continental customers.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}BR’s two-axle Ferry Vans were built for interoperation with european service via cross-channel ferry services, allowing British companies to send goods directly to continental customers.

name: VDA van
bio: The VDA was BR's first air braked general purpose van, later used for the Speedlink freight network.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The VDA was BR's first air braked general purpose van, later used for the Speedlink freight network.

name: Cargowaggon IWB Van
bio: The prominent CARGOWAGGON-lettered vans began arriving in international traffic during the late 1970s and 1980s. These large goods wagons were reliable and high-capacity. The whole hood lifts and slides to permit forklift access to the wagon.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The prominent CARGOWAGGON-lettered vans began arriving in international traffic during the late 1970s and 1980s. These large goods wagons were reliable and high-capacity. The whole hood lifts and slides to permit forklift access to the wagon.

name: VEA van
bio: VEA vans were refurbished vans fitted with improved suspension and air brakes, allowing higher speed operation while still being suitable for narrow radius curves in small yards.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}VEA vans were refurbished vans fitted with improved suspension and air brakes, allowing higher speed operation while still being suitable for narrow radius curves in small yards.

name: VGA van
bio: VGA vans followed continental practice with doors extending into the roof panel, providing a larger vertical opening.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}VGA vans followed continental practice with doors extending into the roof panel, providing a larger vertical opening.

name: Cargowaggon IZA Twin Van
bio: Built as permanently-coupled pairs of two-axle wagons, supposedly to capture an EU subsidy, these smaller cargowaggon sets continue to see service within the UK.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.0 tiles{}{SILVER}Built as permanently-coupled pairs of two-axle wagons, supposedly to capture an EU subsidy, these smaller cargowaggon sets continue to see service within the UK.

name: GWR "Siphon G" van
bio: GWR's "Siphon" vans were built to carry loads of milk churns at passenger train speeds. They were also used for parcel and newspaper traffic, the basic design being built well into the BR era.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{SILVER}GWR's "Siphon" vans were built to carry loads of milk churns at passenger train speeds. They were also used for parcel and newspaper traffic, the basic design being built well into the BR era.

name: Parcels van
bio: BR built several of these six-wheeled parcels vans in the early 1950s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}BR built several of these six-wheeled parcels vans in the early 1950s.

name: Torpedo car
bio: Torpedo cars haul molten metal directly from furnaces, meaning it does not have to be processed to an intermediate form and melted again.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Torpedo cars haul molten metal directly from furnaces, meaning it does not have to be processed to an intermediate form and melted again.

name: Brake wagon
bio: Early brake wagons were little more than open wagons with basic friction brakes added.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{YELLOW}Speed bonus for slow wagons{}{SILVER}Early brake wagons were little more than open wagons with basic friction brakes added.

name: 10t brake van
bio: Brake vans gave extra braking power to allow trains to run at higher speed, and also gave a place for a guard to look after the train from the rear.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{YELLOW}Speed bonus for slow wagons{}{SILVER}Brake vans gave extra braking power to allow trains to run at higher speed, and also gave a place for a guard to look after the train from the rear.

name: Toad 20t brake van
bio: The Great Western Railway standardised brake van designs from 1894. The distinctive design includes an open "veranda" at the back of the vehicle.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}Speed bonus for slow wagons{}{SILVER}The Great Western Railway standardised brake van designs from 1894. The distinctive design includes an open "veranda" at the back of the vehicle.

name: Midland Railway Brake Van
bio: This Midland Railway design of Brake Van was also built by the LMS - over 800 were constructed and they were still extant into the early 1960s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}This Midland Railway design of Brake Van was also built by the LMS - over 800 were constructed and they were still extant into the early 1960s.

name: SR “Queen Mary” Brake Van
bio: The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses.

name: SR “Queen Mary” Brake Van (Alternate)
bio: The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses. This variant is in the alternate company colour scheme.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses. This variant is in the alternate company colour scheme.

name: LMS Brake Van
bio: The native design of LMS Brake Van had a sizable veranda at both ends. Once again, they lasted well into BR ownership as part of Engineers’ trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The native design of LMS Brake Van had a sizable veranda at both ends. Once again, they lasted well into BR ownership as part of Engineers’ trains.

name: Standard 20t brake van
bio: BR inherited a large number of brake vans from the Big Four, but quickly set about introducing their own standard model based on 1940s LNER practice.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}Speed bonus for slow wagons{}{SILVER}BR inherited a large number of brake vans from the Big Four, but quickly set about introducing their own standard model based on 1940s LNER practice.

name: Preserved Clerestory carriage
bio: The first standard gauge preservation railway in the UK was the Bluebell Railway, opened in 1960. Preservation lines are limited to 25mph.
brake_text: {YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}{SILVER}The first standard gauge preservation railway in the UK was the Bluebell Railway, opened in 1960. Preservation lines are limited to 25mph.

name: Preserved BR Mk1 carriage
bio: The ubiquity of the BR Mk1 carriage has made it a mainstay of preserved railways across the UK.
brake_text: {YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}{SILVER}The ubiquity of the BR Mk1 carriage has made it a mainstay of preserved railways across the UK.

name: Metropolitan-Vickers Bo-Bo Locomotive
additional_text: Err:502

name: LU Q Stock
additional_text: Err:502

name: LOR Electric Units
bio: These lightweight multiple units were built for the Liverpool Overhead Railway, a since closed elevated railway operating around the Liverpool docks.
additional_text: #N/A

name: LU 1983 Stock
additional_text: Err:502

name: Pony
bio: A smaller horse for a smaller railway.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}A smaller horse for a smaller railway.

name: Trevithick Tender
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.2 tiles{}{SILVER}

name: Trevithick Locomotive
bio: Trevithick's "Pen-Y-Darren" locomotive was built for a experimental bet that one of Trevithick's stationary engines could pull a train were it mounted on wheels. While the bet was won, it cracked rails intended only for horse-drawn wagons and was returned to service as a stationary engine afterward.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Trevithick's "Pen-Y-Darren" locomotive was built for a experimental bet that one of Trevithick's stationary engines could pull a train were it mounted on wheels. While the bet was won, it cracked rails intended only for horse-drawn wagons and was returned to service as a stationary engine afterward.

name: Princess Tender
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.2 tiles{}{SILVER}

name: George England "Princess"
bio: "Princess" was the first locomotive on the Ffestiniog Railway, going through multiple rebuilds and serving on the railway until its eventual closure in 1946.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}"Princess" was the first locomotive on the Ffestiniog Railway, going through multiple rebuilds and serving on the railway until its eventual closure in 1946.

name: Fletcher Jennings "Talyllyn"
bio: "Talyllyn" was delivered to the Talyllyn railway as an 0-4-0, but converted to 0-4-2 after a few years to improve riding quality. It was retired in 1945, but refurbished in the late 1950s and returned to preservation service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}"Talyllyn" was delivered to the Talyllyn railway as an 0-4-0, but converted to 0-4-2 after a few years to improve riding quality. It was retired in 1945, but refurbished in the late 1950s and returned to preservation service.

name: Fletcher Jennings "Dolgoch"
bio: "Dolgoch" was the second locomotive built for the Talyllyn railway. The heavier and more powerful "Talyllyn" was used more often by the railway, meaning "Dolgoch" was in better condition when preservation began and was the sole running locomotive at first.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}"Dolgoch" was the second locomotive built for the Talyllyn railway. The heavier and more powerful "Talyllyn" was used more often by the railway, meaning "Dolgoch" was in better condition when preservation began and was the sole running locomotive at first.

name: Double Fairlie
bio: The double Fairlie design is iconic of the Ffestiniog Railway. After the success of the first, "Little Wonder", the railway continued to build Fairlie locomotives under licence. This continues into modern times with the latest, "James Spooner II" due for completion in 2021.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The double Fairlie design is iconic of the Ffestiniog Railway. After the success of the first, "Little Wonder", the railway continued to build Fairlie locomotives under licence. This continues into modern times with the latest, "James Spooner II" due for completion in 2021.

name: Hunslet 0-4-0ST "Alice"
bio: These small Hunslet tank engines were used as quarry locomotives. "Alice" worked at Dinorwic, being used as a source of spare parts afterward, before being restored in the late '80s and early '90s and now running on the Bala Lake Railway.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}These small Hunslet tank engines were used as quarry locomotives. "Alice" worked at Dinorwic, being used as a source of spare parts afterward, before being restored in the late '80s and early '90s and now running on the Bala Lake Railway.

name: Hudswell Clarke 4-8-0
additional_text: Err:502

name: Barclay Sons & Co. E Class
bio: The E Class is a small 0-4-0 well tank. One used by the RAF is preserved on the Talyllyn railway as "Douglas", named after second owner Douglas Abelson who donated it from his engineering firm.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The E Class is a small 0-4-0 well tank. One used by the RAF is preserved on the Talyllyn railway as "Douglas", named after second owner Douglas Abelson who donated it from his engineering firm.

name: GWR Rheidol Tank
bio: Charles Collett designed these tank locomotives to replace worn-out engines on the Vale of Rheidol line. They became the last steam locomotives running on British Railways, up until the line became a heritage railway in 1989.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Charles Collett designed these tank locomotives to replace worn-out engines on the Vale of Rheidol line. They became the last steam locomotives running on British Railways, up until the line became a heritage railway in 1989.

name: Ruston and Hornsby 33/40HP
additional_text: Err:502

name: Ruston and Hornsby 44/48HP
bio: Ruston and Hornsby built diesel locomotives for small industrial lines, which would often only need a small locomotive for hauling goods short distances.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Ruston and Hornsby built diesel locomotives for small industrial lines, which would often only need a small locomotive for hauling goods short distances.

name: Hunslet 0-4-0DM
bio: The Hunslet 0-4-0DM is powered by a Gardner 4LW engine and is typical of a narrow gauge locomotive used for engineering duties.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}The Hunslet 0-4-0DM is powered by a Gardner 4LW engine and is typical of a narrow gauge locomotive used for engineering duties.

name: John Bate "Toby"
bio: Talyllyn Railway volunteer John Bate built this small locomotive using the engine from a Austin Seven. The canopy was added later.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Talyllyn Railway volunteer John Bate built this small locomotive using the engine from a Austin Seven. The canopy was added later.

name: Baguley-Drewry Diesel
bio: Larger and more powerful diesel shunters displaced steam from industrial lines as they were more economical to run. Even preservation railways keep some around for utility duties.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Larger and more powerful diesel shunters displaced steam from industrial lines as they were more economical to run. Even preservation railways keep some around for utility duties.

name: Incline car
bio: Early narrow gauge railways were used solely for freight, until the Ffestiniog Railway began carrying passengers in the 1860s. Early gravity railways used incline cars to deliver quarry workers swiftly to their place of work.
brake_text: {YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}{SILVER}Early narrow gauge railways were used solely for freight, until the Ffestiniog Railway began carrying passengers in the 1860s. Early gravity railways used incline cars to deliver quarry workers swiftly to their place of work.

name: Four-wheel carriage
bio: When narrow gauge began operating passenger services, carriages were simple, flat-sided vehicles build around wooden frames on a four-wheel chassis.
brake_text: {YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}{SILVER}When narrow gauge began operating passenger services, carriages were simple, flat-sided vehicles build around wooden frames on a four-wheel chassis.

name: Bogie carriage
bio: Later narrow gauge carriages increased in length where loading gauges allowed, using bogies to help negotiate tight curves.
brake_text: {YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}Preserved railway bonus: passengers pay more when hauled by old locomotives{}{SILVER}Later narrow gauge carriages increased in length where loading gauges allowed, using bogies to help negotiate tight curves.

name: Four-wheel brake carriage
bio: Brake vans were rarer on narrow gauge railways, but passenger services would typically have a guard's wagon which could also carry parcels.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Brake vans were rarer on narrow gauge railways, but passenger services would typically have a guard's wagon which could also carry parcels.

name: Bogie brake carriage
bio: Larger and heavier carriages also meant larger and heavier guard's vans.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}Larger and heavier carriages also meant larger and heavier guard's vans.

name: Open wagon
bio: An open wagon for carrying bulk cargo on narrow gauge railways.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}An open wagon for carrying bulk cargo on narrow gauge railways.

name: Mineral wagon
bio: Heavier mineral wagons allowed larger amounts of cargo to be carried.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Heavier mineral wagons allowed larger amounts of cargo to be carried.

name: Flat wagon
bio: While many narrow gauge railways were quarry lines carrying bulk goods or slate, flat wagons were used for transporting equipment.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}While many narrow gauge railways were quarry lines carrying bulk goods or slate, flat wagons were used for transporting equipment.

name: Bolster wagon
bio: As per standard gauge practice, bolster wagons were used for carrying long, rigid cargo.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}As per standard gauge practice, bolster wagons were used for carrying long, rigid cargo.

name: Long Bolster
bio: Narrow gauge railways tend to use smaller vehicles capable of traversing tight turns, but the occasional larger wagon can be found on straighter routes.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}Narrow gauge railways tend to use smaller vehicles capable of traversing tight turns, but the occasional larger wagon can be found on straighter routes.

name: Tank wagon
bio: With specialised equipment at stations, tank wagons made liquid cargo easier to handle than loading drums into open wagons.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}With specialised equipment at stations, tank wagons made liquid cargo easier to handle than loading drums into open wagons.

name: Livestock van
bio: A van for carrying livestock on narrow gauge railways.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}A van for carrying livestock on narrow gauge railways.

name: Wooden-framed van
bio: Like their larger counterparts, narrow gauge railways built many vans for carrying piece goods.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Like their larger counterparts, narrow gauge railways built many vans for carrying piece goods.

name: Gunpowder van
bio: Gunpowder vans carried explosives to the quarries served by narrow gauge railways. This example has solid iron construction.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Gunpowder vans carried explosives to the quarries served by narrow gauge railways. This example has solid iron construction.

name: Iron-framed van
bio: Later vans used iron frames to increase their strength and enable running at faster speeds.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Later vans used iron frames to increase their strength and enable running at faster speeds.

name: Dandy wagon
bio: Early gravity railways sent cargo downhill with horses riding in dandy wagons. At the bottom the horses would be unloaded and used to haul the empty train back up the hill.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.3 tiles{}{SILVER}Early gravity railways sent cargo downhill with horses riding in dandy wagons. At the bottom the horses would be unloaded and used to haul the empty train back up the hill.
